entries.title: North Center mural depicting Ukrainian refugee defaced
entries.summary: <p>A controversial North Center mural of a Ukrainian refugee who was stabbed to death last summer was vandalized.</p><p>Iryna Zarutska, a 23-year-old Ukrainian refugee, is depicted in the mural from the shoulders up, wearing a blue shirt and soft gaze on her face. Her name is written in white above the dates 2002-2025. Zarutska was fatally stabbed last summer while riding a train home from work in Charlotte, North Carolina.</p><p>Republicans, including President Donald Trump and Vice President JD Vance, have blamed Democrats’ “soft-on-crime” policies for the stabbing. The man charged in Zarutska’s death has a record of robbery and had been arrested 14 times in the last 10 years.</p><p>The mural, along with similar ones in major cities around the U.S., was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/01/27/mural-of-slain-ukrainian-refugee-effort-funded-by-elon-musk-appears-in-chicago-sav45" target="_blank" >part of a campaign backed by Elon Musk</a> and launched by Eoghan McCabe, CEO of artificial intelligence service Intercom.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-db0000" name="image-db0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d1f7e9a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa4%2Fe3%2F0bf979e14a178534b30913e8eeae%2Fmuskmural-260124-03.jpg alt="A mural of Iryna Zarutska at 2415 W Montrose Ave in North Center,, Friday, Jan. 23, 2026. | Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/182d5fa/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa4%2Fe3%2F0bf979e14a178534b30913e8eeae%2Fmuskmural-260124-03.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1534cc7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa4%2Fe3%2F0bf979e14a178534b30913e8eeae%2Fmuskmural-260124-03.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/182d5fa/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa4%2Fe3%2F0bf979e14a178534b30913e8eeae%2Fmuskmural-260124-03.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A mural of Iryna Zarutska near the corner of Western and Montrose avenues in North Center seen on Jan. 23, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>As of Monday evening, the center of the mural was dotted with black splotches of paint.</p><p>Even before it was defaced, the mural received mixed reviews from the Ukrainian community. Some saw the mural, near the intersection of Montrose and Western, as a welcome homage to Zarutska and other Ukrainian refugees.</p><p>“It’s good to keep this memory of this innocent girl,” Halyna Parasiuk, an archivist at the Ukrainian National Museum of Chicago, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/01/27/mural-of-slain-ukrainian-refugee-effort-funded-by-elon-musk-appears-in-chicago-sav45" target="_blank" >previously told the Chicago Sun-Times</a>.</p><p>But others felt the mural was misguided and in poor taste. Zarutska’s family wasn’t asked permission to display her face and name prominently on a building, according to Mariya Dmytriv-Kapeniak, president of the Ukrainian Congress Committee of America Illinois chapter, who said she’s connected to Zarutska’s community in North Carolina.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/01/27/mural-of-slain-ukrainian-refugee-effort-funded-by-elon-musk-appears-in-chicago-sav45" target="_blank" >Mural of slain Ukrainian refugee, linked to effort funded by Elon Musk, appears in Chicago</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/02/05/local-ukrainian-group-fighting-to-keep-murals-of-slain-woman-out-of-chicago-iryna-zarutska" target="_blank" >Local Ukrainian group fighting to keep murals of slain woman out of Chicago</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>“If someone really cares about war refugees, there’s a lot of work you can do to help them, other than to paint a mural without family’s permission and just sign a poor girl’s name on it,” Dmytriv-Kapeniak said last month.</p><p>The Ukrainian National Museum of Chicago and Dmytriv-Kapeniak didn’t respond to requests for comment on the mural being defaced.</p><p>A similar mural was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/02/05/local-ukrainian-group-fighting-to-keep-murals-of-slain-woman-out-of-chicago-iryna-zarutska" target="_blank" >requested by a resident of the 36th Ward</a>, Ald. Gilbert Villegas told the Ukrainian Congress Committee of America. Villegas’ office didn’t respond to requests for comment, and it’s unclear where that proposal stands and if another mural will be painted.</p><p>The owner of the building that displays the mural and artist of the mural, known as Sav45, didn’t respond to requests for comment.</p>
entries.author: Mary Norkol

entries.title: The Rev. Jesse Jackson comes home, to the South Carolina people that molded the man Chicago knew
entries.summary: <p>GREENVILLE, S.C. — Back in 1960, the story goes, a teenaged Jesse Jackson entered the whites-only branch of his hometown library with seven comrades-in-arms who’d be christened the Greenville Eight after getting arrested for their civil disobedience.</p><p>It was the first of the civil rights icon’s many arrests, at 19, as part of the NAACP’s youth council at the end of his freshman year at the flagship University of Illinois campus.</p><p>But the young people who sat down in the well-appointed white library in July 1960 got up and left when staffers shut off the lights. They returned to their advisor, the Rev. James S. Hall, at the Springfield Baptist Church. He promptly sent them back.</p><p>“They said to me, ‘They were going to put us in jail’,” recalls Hall, now 93. “I said, ‘That's what I sent you there for.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-floatLeft> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-fb0000" name="image-fb0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8df00b6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd2%2Fa3%2F35cd97a446528438948a67ac671a%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-80.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e8fedbd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd2%2Fa3%2F35cd97a446528438948a67ac671a%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-80.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5d1d928/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd2%2Fa3%2F35cd97a446528438948a67ac671a%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-80.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="The Rev. James S. Hall inside the South Carolina Capitol Building on Monday in Columbia, SC. Hall helped launch and shape Jackson's activism when he was a teenager in Greenville, SC. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5d1d928/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd2%2Fa3%2F35cd97a446528438948a67ac671a%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-80.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/39a5725/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd2%2Fa3%2F35cd97a446528438948a67ac671a%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-80.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5d1d928/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd2%2Fa3%2F35cd97a446528438948a67ac671a%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-80.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Rev. James S. Hall inside the South Carolina Capitol Building on Monday in Columbia, SC. Hall helped launch and shape Jackson’s activism when he was a teenager in Greenville, SC. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>For most of his 84 years, the Rev. Jackson powered through so many high places, organizing boycotts, freeing hostages and launching two presidential bids, that it begs remembering he wasn’t born a giant.<b> </b>And despite living most of his life in Chicago, that is not where his story began.</p><p>The son of a teenaged mother, Jackson died Feb. 17 and lay in honor on Monday in the Capitol rotunda of the state he thought of as home and returned to throughout the rest of his life.</p><p>Before he landed in Chicago in his 20s to establish his own power, starting with Dr. Martin Luther King Jr., activism was something Jackson had to learn from those around him, from mentors and teachers who nurtured and bolstered him and his classmates from Sterling High School, once an institution for Black Greenville.</p><p>“We had wonderful, underscore, wonderful teachers who believed in you,” says Dorris Wright, another of the Greenville Eight, who went on to make her own mark in the Civil Rights Movement as part of incidents that led to two U.S. Supreme Court rulings. “One English teacher had a Ph.D., “and we asked, ‘Why are you here?’ And she said, ‘this is where I'm needed.”</p><div class="Enhancement" " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/92bdf37/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffe%2F99%2F3aea66fe42f582e4a9e50a69856d%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-67.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/70163c8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffe%2F99%2F3aea66fe42f582e4a9e50a69856d%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-67.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/92bdf37/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffe%2F99%2F3aea66fe42f582e4a9e50a69856d%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-67.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Dorris Wright, one of the “Greenville Eight” who integrated the Greenville public library in 1960 alongside a young Jesse Jackson, at the South Carolina Capitol Building on Monday. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“You didn't have a lot to distract you growing up in the segregated South,” Wright says. “But most of our activities were centered around high school.</p><p>“You knew you were loved, both at home and in school. So I mean, it was just something that it's not verbal, I would say it's visceral. It was something that you felt and the teachers always encouraged you to continue to go on.”</p><p>Then Greenville’s young people like Wright and Jackson began sitting in at lunch counters and parks. Their youth let them draw attention that would have cost their parents their jobs.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <bsp-carousel class="Carousel Enhancement" data-align-center> <a class="AnchorLink" id="gallery-780001" name="gallery-780001"></a> <div> <div class="Carousel-slides"> <div class="Carousel-slide"> <div class="CarouselSlide" > <div class="CarouselSlide-media"> <picture data-crop="large"> <source type="image/webp" width="840" height="555" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e0eb3dd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1000x661+0+358/resize/840x555!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F9a%2Fd5b64fa84ea58e5d90be6181433e%2Fp17168coll68-1356-large.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/eb160b9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1000x661+0+358/resize/1680x1110!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F9a%2Fd5b64fa84ea58e5d90be6181433e%2Fp17168coll68-1356-large.jpg 2x"/> <source width="840" height="555" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4380698/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1000x661+0+358/resize/840x555!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F03%2F9a%2Fd5b64fa84ea58e5d90be6181433e%2Fp17168coll68-1356-large.jpg"/> <img class="Image" alt="Picture of the Rev. Jesse Jackson's high school yearbook showing him in his football gear with his coach." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2ecbcb2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1000x1000+0+358/resize/300x300!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3a%2F5a%2F44355c8e449bb6cd9bbd368bac29%2Fp17168coll68-1301-large.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fbd9d9d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1000x1000+0+358/resize/600x600!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3a%2F5a%2F44355c8e449bb6cd9bbd368bac29%2Fp17168coll68-1301-large.jpg 2x" width="300" height="300" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2ecbcb2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1000x1000+0+358/resize/300x300!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3a%2F5a%2F44355c8e449bb6cd9bbd368bac29%2Fp17168coll68-1301-large.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIzMDBweCIgd2lkdGg9IjMwMHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> </span> </div> </div> </div></div><div class="FlickityCustomNavigation-wrapper"> <div class="FlickityCustomNavigation below"> <button class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-prev" type="button" aria-label="Previous"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-left"></use></svg> </button> <button class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-next" type="button" aria-label="Next"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-right"></use></svg> </button> </div> </div></div> </bsp-carousel> </div> </div><p></p><p>Jackson signed Wright’s yearbook in June 1959, with the inscription, “Dear Dorris you possess the ability of a wonderful leader. Use it wisely. A pal always, Jesse.” He’d later write the prologue to her 2022 book, “The (W)right Thing: My Life in the Civil Rights Movement.”</p><p>“It’s so interesting, he would say, ‘You possess the ability of a wonderful leader,” she says. “And he turned out to be that leader.”</p><h3>‘Jesse, promise me you’ll be somebody’</h3><p>Jackson was born to a single young mom, “and his biological father, who lived just down the street, I guess you would say, really never gave him the time of the day,” recalls James Felder, who was 14 when he befriended a 12-year-old Jackson. “But his stepfather still stood in and Jesse got his moorings, I guess you would say, from his stepfather, whose name was [Charles] Jackson.”</p><p>Jackson, who excelled at sports, had other mentors, too.</p><p>“His coach — he always talked about Coach [Joseph D.] Mathis — that was the man in his life, and there were a couple others,” says Felder, one of the first Black people elected to the South Carolina legislature. Jackson “as they say, he had a lot of spunk, and they saw something in him. So they kept encouraging him.”</p><p>Jackson’s mother, Helen Burns Jackson, eventually became a cosmetologist, so friends remember young Jesse Jackson spending time with her mother, Matilda Burns, who adored him.</p><p>In his 1986 autobiography “Up with Hope,” Jesse Jackson wrote that his grandmother never learned to read herself, but appreciated books and education, and wanted more for her grandson. “When God made you, he did his best work,” she had once said. “Jesse, promise me you’ll be somebody.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item"> <bsp-carousel class="Carousel Enhancement" data-align-center> <a class="AnchorLink" id="gallery-7e0001" name="gallery-7e0001"></a> <div> <div class="Carousel-slides"> <div class="Carousel-slide"> <div class="CarouselSlide" > <div class="CarouselSlide-media"> <picture data-crop="large"> <source type="image/webp" width="840" height="555" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3a6f21a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1982+0+7/resize/840x555!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F37%2F7d%2F348c669d48f88848019869b3a293%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-12.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5c2e85c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1982+0+7/resize/1680x1110!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F37%2F7d%2F348c669d48f88848019869b3a293%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-12.jpg 2x"/> <source width="840" height="555" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d1909a9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1982+0+7/resize/840x555!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F37%2F7d%2F348c669d48f88848019869b3a293%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-12.jpg"/> <img class="Image" alt="Members of the South Carolina Highway Patrol help carry the Rev. Jesse Jackson lay in honor in South Carolina, where he he was born and raised. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2983ef8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2000x2000+500+0/resize/300x300!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F82%2F42%2F03e839404e16b96093183a8725b4%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-30.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b377b17/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2000x2000+500+0/resize/600x600!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F82%2F42%2F03e839404e16b96093183a8725b4%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-30.jpg 2x" width="300" height="300" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2983ef8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2000x2000+500+0/resize/300x300!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F82%2F42%2F03e839404e16b96093183a8725b4%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-30.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIzMDBweCIgd2lkdGg9IjMwMHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> </span> </div> </div> </div></div><div class="FlickityCustomNavigation-wrapper"> <div class="FlickityCustomNavigation below"> <button class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-prev" type="button" aria-label="Previous"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-left"></use></svg> </button> <button class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-next" type="button" aria-label="Next"> <svg class="FlickityCustomNavigation-button-icon"><use xlink:href="#icon-right"></use></svg> </button> </div> </div></div> </bsp-carousel> </div> </div><p>On Monday, South Carolina sent Jackson off with high honors, beneath the dome of its copper-domed statehouse. Draped in a flag, Jackson’s casket processed around the Capitol on a horse-drawn carriage, his children following on foot. The state police who carried the casket wore ceremonial dress; on the steps outside, a choir sang a traditional Chicago funeral hymn, “Take My Hand, Precious Lord.”</p><p>Sprays of flowers announced “Son of South Carolina.” Meanwhile, lines of people waited for hours for the public’s turn to walk slowly past his casket.</p><p>Greenville’s state representative Chandra Dillard — who says she and Jackson called each other “Greenville homeboys”— thanked his children for “bringing their dad back to South Carolina.”</p><p>Such a grand homecoming for a Black man was unthinkable in Jackson's earliest days.</p><p>Greenville’s brand of racism stood out in the Jim Crow South. As Black people pushed to integrate the city pool, news accounts reported at the time, the city council voted to convert it into a marine park, complete with sea lions, rather than let Black children swim there.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-740000" name="image-740000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2938060/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0b%2F70%2F5882838d408f83196a04998843a6%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-106.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/78009a0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0b%2F70%2F5882838d408f83196a04998843a6%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-106.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0bc9b48/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0b%2F70%2F5882838d408f83196a04998843a6%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-106.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A sign showing a street named in honor of the Rev. Jesse Jackson in his hometown of Greenville, SC on Monday, March 2." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0bc9b48/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0b%2F70%2F5882838d408f83196a04998843a6%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-106.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bc102b0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0b%2F70%2F5882838d408f83196a04998843a6%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-106.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0bc9b48/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F0b%2F70%2F5882838d408f83196a04998843a6%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-106.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A street honors the Rev. Jesse Jackson in his hometown of Greenville, SC on Monday, March 2.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>A green marker now announces the bumpy block of Haynie Street as “Reverend Jesse L. Jackson Sr. Street.” But nothing identifies the little white-sided house as the one where Jackson was born, to a teenaged mother who couldn’t afford a hospital stay.</p><p>That house wouldn’t have an indoor toilet for more than a decade after his birth, says Freddie Clinkscales, 84, whose family bought the home a little before she started at Sterling High School, graduating with Jackson in 1959.</p><p>“When we moved there we had to go outside and use the bathroom,” she says. “And my parents added on two more rooms and a bathroom to be on the inside of the house.”</p><p>It’s still barely 1,200 square feet, obviously one of the three oldest houses on the block amid a slew of new construction booming in Greenville.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div Jesse Jackson was born and spent his early years. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Connected to Greenville for life</h3><p>Jackson’s Greenville friends marveled at how he made the time to stay in touch.</p><p>The Rev. Hall was just 25 when he was sent to pastor Greenville’s large Black church, and he and his family hosted the youth council. The family’s commitment to the NAACP also included taking in baseball legend Jackie Robinson when Greenville hotels refused him service, and their help getting Robinson back to the airport led to an effort to desegregate airport waiting rooms.</p><p>“It was then that we decided that we would march on the airport, and Jesse Jackson was a part of that. And he then became involved in the movement of the young people when they were sitting in at lunch counters and in the library,” Hall recalls.</p><p>“We just kept the fact going because of what my father taught me, and what I believed and what I read in the Bible about what God intended for us to be and not what the racist country intended to make us: What I am right now, Baptist preacher, but one who is not afraid.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ff0000" " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bb80ab8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd0%2Fa6%2Fa6e4eaab4628843155ee21c253f2%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-119.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/aa6dbc0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd0%2Fa6%2Fa6e4eaab4628843155ee21c253f2%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-119.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bb80ab8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd0%2Fa6%2Fa6e4eaab4628843155ee21c253f2%2Fjjsouthcarolina-260303-119.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Springfield Baptist Church, Jesse Jackson’s home church in Greenville that helped inspire his activism. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Their friendship continued after the Halls moved to Philadelphia, and years later, between his 1984 and 1988 presidential campaigns, Jackson would officiate the wedding of Hall’s daughter.</p><p>On the Greenville Eight’s 40th anniversary, Jackson also brought some of them to Chicago for a celebration. Wright was among them, and on Monday, she dressed up and waited her turn in the line outside the Capitol to say goodbye to her friend.</p><p>“It was how he made you feel,” Wright says. “And then when he coined the phrase, ‘I am somebody,’ I think it was carrying on what he had felt was who he was. He was somebody and he was loved.”</p><p><i>Contributing: Somer Van Benton</i> </p>
entries.author: Lauren FitzPatrick

entries.title: Five storylines to watch during the IHSA sectionals
entries.summary: <p>With regional play completed, we are now into March and neck-deep into the IHSA state tournament brackets.</p><p>Here are five storylines to keep an eye on in this week’s sectional action.</p><h3>Can underdogs continue to surprise?</h3><h3></h3><p>As the No. 13 seed with upsets over No. 4 Naperville North and No. 5 Downers Grove South, Yorkville is the lowest seeded team to advance to sectional play in several years.</p><p>Simeon, the No. 11 seed in the Mount Carmel Sectional, took down No. 6 Riverside-Brookfield and No. 3 Young to keep its regional championship streak intact.</p><p>But what are the chances either of these underdogs can pull off another upset and play for a sectional championship on Friday night?</p><p>For Yorkville? Not so good. The surging Foxes will enter Tuesday’s game with No. 1 ranked Benet as a massive underdog.</p><p>Simeon will face a tall task as well but more manageable in facing St. Ignatius.</p><h3>Sophomore stars collide again</h3><h3></h3><p>The annual When Sides Collide in January provided a glimpse at the top two sophomore prospects in the state. Neuqua Valley’s Cole Kelly and Bolingbrook’s Brady Pettigrew squared off in a marquee matchup and didn’t disappoint.</p><p>Kelly, who scored 29 points and pulled down 11 rebounds, led Neuqua to a 75-69 win. Pettigrew finished with 26 points in the loss.</p><p>A little over a month later the two have led their respective teams to the sectional semifinals where they will meet again in a star-studded night at Bolingbrook on Wednesday.</p><p>High school basketball rarity: 2,000-point scorers meet</p><p>A rare occurrence will take place Tuesday night in the Rockton-Hononegah Sectional.</p><p>A pair of players, both with over 2,000 points in their careers, will meet in the postseason. Warren junior star Jaxson Davis and Rockford Auburn senior Amir Danforth have both surpassed the impressive point total.</p><p>The last time it occurred — and possibly the only time? — was in 2015 when Lake Forest’s Evan Boudreaux and Stevenson’s Jalen Brunson met in the sectional championship.</p><h3>Stars showcased in 3A sectional</h3><p>Folks in Ottawa probably have no rooting interest in Wednesday’s sectional semifinal matchup between Kankakee and East Peoria. Neither school is within 60 miles of the sectional host school. But area basketball fans attending will be rewarded as two of the state’s top talents take the floor.</p><p>Kankakee’s Lincoln Williams is the No. 2 ranked prospect in the Class of 2026 and the best uncommitted senior in the state. The versatile 6-6 Williams has averaged 26 points and six rebounds a game while leading the Kays to a 28-1 record.</p><p>East Peoria’s Quinton Kitt is the No. 2 ranked prospect in the Class of 2027 and committed to Illinois. With 37 points in the regional final, Kitt led East Peoria to its first regional championship since 1997.</p><h3>Can Kaneland get to 34-0?</h3><p>The last time an unbeaten made it to the IHSA State Finals in either of the two largest classes was Fremd’s 2017 team. The Vikings rolled into Peoria with a 30-0 record before losing to Young in the state semifinals.</p><p>Unbeaten Kaneland is three wins away.</p><p>The Knights, now 32-0 after two convincing regional wins, will be considerable favorites in the sectional semifinals against Rockford East (16-17). If Sycamore gets by Crystal Lake South, it would set up a third matchup of the season between Sycamore and Kaneland.</p><p>The state hasn’t had an undefeated state champion since Seneca’s 35-0 season in 2006 ended with a Class A state title.</p>
entries.author: Joe Henricksen

entries.title: Blackhawks trade Connor Murphy to Oilers, ending his nine-year run in Chicago
entries.summary: <p>WINNIPEG, Manitoba — Nine years ago, former <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> general manager Stan Bowman made one of his few smart moves of the post-Stanley Cup era by acquiring Connor Murphy from the Coyotes for Niklas Hjalmarsson in a swap of defensemen.</p><p>On Monday, Bowman — now the GM of the Oilers — acquired Murphy again, putting an end to his lengthy stint with the Hawks four days before the NHL trade deadline Friday. It was an on-brand move by Bowman, who historically loved reacquiring former players.</p><p>The Hawks received a second-round draft pick in 2028 from the Oilers for Murphy, a pending free agent. They retained half his $4.4 million salary-cap hit, leaving them with one remaining retention slot this season.</p><p>A second-round pick always seemed to be the best realistic return, so Hawks GM Kyle Davidson should be pleased with that. </p><p>It also makes sense for the Hawks to target years-away picks. They could use that 2028 pick, for example, as a trade chip of their own once they start contending and pivot from sellers to buyers.</p><p>Murphy had seen the writing on the wall for months. He recently admitted to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/02/26/blackhawks-connor-murphy-nhl-trade-deadline-rumors-playoffs-allure" target="_blank" >feeling the allure</a> of the playoffs, having never competed in a real postseason (only in the COVID bubble) as his 33rd birthday approaches this month.</p><p>He won’t get a cakewalk to that stage with the Oilers, but he will have a good chance to make it — especially because his presence should improve their defense, which has been a major weakness this season.</p><p>At 29-24-8, the Oilers — the back-to-back Western Conference champions — actually have lost more games than they’ve won, but they still occupy a wild-card spot (based on points percentage) at the moment.</p><p>They needed another right-handed defenseman, and Murphy will slide just below Evan Bouchard on that side of their depth chart. They’re more set on the left side with Darnell Nurse, Mattias Ekholm and Jake Walman.</p><p>For the Hawks, Murphy’s departure should allow rookies Artyom Levshunov and Sam Rinzel to stay in the NHL lineup consistently down the stretch, even when Wyatt Kaiser returns Friday from his knee injury.</p><p>Matt Grzelcyk, another pending free agent, is the Hawks’ only veteran left on defense. Next season, the eldest statesman might be Alex Vlasic, even though he won’t turn 25 until June.</p><p>Murphy boosted his value with strong performances in the last couple of months, playing his usual solid defense and building up a rare bit of offensive confidence. His ice time bottomed out in early November, then increased steadily after that.</p><p>He has stayed healthy this season after missing extended time each of the last two seasons because of complications stemming from a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2025/01/29/blackhawks-connor-murphy-osteitis-pubis-groin-condition-logan-couture-nhl-injury" target="_blank" >groin condition</a>.</p><p>He’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/01/28/blackhawks-connor-murphy-reflects-on-tenure-chicago-home-patience" target="_blank" >vocal about his love</a> for Chicago and has been heavily involved in community-service initiatives, so it wouldn’t be surprising if he moved back to the city once he retires.</p><p>‘‘I’ve been here nine years, and I feel like a Blackhawk to the core,’’ Murphy said in January. ‘‘I feel like I’ve gotten so close to the staff and the city. It’s home. It’s where my wife is from. We had our kid and got married here. I’ve always envisioned how unbelievable it would be to have success in a city like this.</p><p>‘‘You want it to be forever, but you know [it won’t be] when you see the reality of other players that have been traded. I’ve prepared myself over the last years of knowing that it is a business and that, someday, that day will come.’’</p><p>Davidson likely isn’t done with moves for the week. Forwards Jason Dickinson and Ilya Mikheyev remain on the block, and captain Nick Foligno still might decide to chase one more playoff run.</p><p>All three of those players are pending free agents, too, and the Hawks have to free up roster spots for forward prospects, as well.</p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" entries.title: Chicago police officer faces firing for shooting 13-year-old boy after mistaking cellphone for gun
entries.summary: <p>A Chicago police officer faces dismissal nearly four years after he shot and paralyzed a 13-year-old boy who was holding a cellphone that was mistaken for a gun.</p><p>It’s the second time the Civilian Office of Police Accountability has pushed to fire Officer Noah Ball over an on-duty shooting. In the earlier case, Ball was given a one-day suspension after Supt. Larry Snelling fought COPA’s recommendation while the second investigation was well underway.</p><p>Ball encountered the teenager late May 18, 2022, when the boy hopped out of a car wanted in a carjacking and kidnapping and ran from pursuing officers, COPA said in a report released last week.</p><p>When the boy reached a gas station in the 800 block of North Cicero Avenue, he turned and appeared to raise both hands, COPA said. Another officer pointed a gun at the boy, but didn’t fire because he didn’t have a “clear visual” of his hands.</p><p>Ball shot three times, COPA said. As officers picked the boy off the ground, a phone in a black case appeared on the ground next to a small pool of blood.</p><p>“It’s a cell phone,” Ball said. “It’s a f----ing cell phone.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/fWBCcgLRQlo?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Video shows Chicago police shoot 13-year-old boy"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>Police said the boy was unarmed, and no shots were fired at officers.</p><p>The boy was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2022/7/21/23272615/chicago-police-shooting-video-13-gas-station" target="_blank" ><u>paralyzed from the waist down</u></a>, according to his family’s attorney. The family filed a federal lawsuit against the city and the Chicago Police Department in October 2022.</p><p>Attorney’s for Ball and the boy’s family didn’t immediately respond to requests for comment.</p><p>The Cook County state’s attorney’s office determined there was “insufficient evidence” to support criminal charges, a spokesperson said.</p><p>COPA found Ball violated department orders by shooting at the boy and failing to turn on his body-worn camera. </p><p>In a letter to COPA Chief Administrator LaKenya White, Snelling agreed with the agency’s findings and the recommendation to fire Ball. He also said the police department would request that Ball be placed on the city’s do-not-hire list.</p><p>Snelling previously went to bat for Ball when COPA sought to have him fired for another shooting in West Garfield Park in June 2021. No one was injured during the armed encounter, but COPA investigators found Ball hadn’t faced an “imminent threat” and questioned whether he was fired upon.</p><p>“This misconduct was of the most egregious nature, that which jeopardized the safety of an individual and may have resulted in physical injury or the loss of life,” COPA said.</p><p>Snelling disagreed, though. He said Ball’s force was “objectively reasonable, necessary, and proportional in order to protect Officer Ball and the other officers on scene that day.”</p><p>The disciplinary dispute was later settled by a single member of the Chicago Police Board, which adopted Snelling’s recommendation for a one-day suspension.</p><p>COPA’s attempts to interview Ball about the newer allegations were unsuccessful. He has been stripped of his police powers pending the resolution of his case.</p>
entries.author: Sophie Sherry

entries.title: Unrivaled executive and former Sky coach shed light on Angel Reese's return
entries.summary: <p>NEW YORK — After making a splash in Unrivaled's inaugural season, Angel Reese decided not to return for Year 2. She then made a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2026/02/11/angel-reese-rejoins-3x3-league-unrivaled-with-mid-season-deal" >surprise comeback at the tail end of the season.</a></p><p>Before the Unrivaled semifinals at Barclays Center, league president Alex Bazzell explained how the league maintained its connection with Reese during her absence.</p><p>“We knew that this year there were going to be some issues with her calendar,” Bazzell said. “Her shoe release came out. She went overseas, so there was a constant dialogue of could we make it work earlier in the season or not?”</p><p>Bazzell said Reese didn’t want to be a distraction by coming in and out of the league while juggling other commitments, so she ultimately chose not to play. But when injuries hit Rose, a need opened up. The team also was looking to bolster its roster ahead of a playoff push.</p><p>So Reese returned, with a nudge from her former Sky coach and Unrivaled Vinyl coach Teresa Weatherspoon.</p><p>“I won’t say that I was the one to convince her but we . . . speak about the game a lot,” Weatherspoon told the Sun-Times before of Vinyl’s semifinal loss to the Phantom. “I know how she’s working in her offseason . . . and as you’re growing your game, live reps are important. That’s all I said. Live reps are important. Get you some live reps if that’s what you wanna do.”</p><p>Live reps were indeed good for Reese, who will suit up for Team USA in the FIBA qualifiers later this month. And they were good for Rose, too. Coming off the bench over four games, Reese averaged a double-double and helped the team qualify for the playoffs.</p><p>For the league as a whole, Year 2 brought cause for both celebration and concern. Hugely successful tour stops in Philadelphia and New York helped grow revenue to $45 million, up from $27 million last year. But television ratings declined and remain well below other women’s basketball properties like the WNBA and college basketball.</p><p>The big question for Unrivaled going forward is whether it can retain its stars, such as Reese. Bazzell reiterated his mission of making the league home for “the best players.”</p><p>Compensation was a major draw in the first place. Reese made more in her debut Unrivaled season than she would have earned over the life of her WNBA rookie contract under the previous CBA.</p><p>But the landscape is shifting quickly. According to the most recent CBA proposals, top players are poised to make more than $1 million in WNBA salary for the first time. Unrivaled will have to be creative in how it retains its stars, who soon will be earning big from both the WNBA and endorsements.</p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-010001" name="list-010001"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> Latest on the Sky and WNBA </div> </div> </div> <div class="PageListH-items" data-with-borders data-list-loadmore-items> <div class="PageListH-items-item"><div class="PagePromoB"> <div class="PagePromo-media"><a class="Link" aria-label="Will there even be a season? entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: Chicago selected as finalist to host Democratic National Convention
entries.summary: <p>Coming off the heels of a massive Democratic National Convention in 2024, Chicago could once again host the event in 2028 and 2032.</p><p>Democratic National Committee announced Monday that Chicago was one of five cities selected as finalists to host the event alongside Atlanta, Boston, Denver and Philadelphia.</p><p>Held before each presidential election, the convention includes speeches from prominent Democrats and the party formally nominates its presidential and vice presidential candidates. Chicago hosted the 2024 Democratic National Convention, when the party nominated former Vice President Kamala Harris and Minnesota Governor Tim Walz to run against now-President Donald Trump and Vice President JD Vance.</p><p>“With our extensive track-record of safely and successfully hosting large-scale events, our vibrant and diverse culture, and our status as a world-class destination — we’re confident that Chicago is best positioned to put on a convention that matches the enthusiasm, success, and energy of the 2024 DNC,” the Chicago Bid Committee said in a statement. “The team that brought Democrats the best political convention is ready to do it again.”</p><p>The Chicago Bid Committee submitted its proposal to host the event earlier this year and met with DNC officials in February to answer questions about the proposal and preparation to hold the event.</p><p>Chicago’s bid video lauds the city as a political leader that is home to hard workers and high-profile Democrats like former President Barack Obama. Oprah Winfrey, Derrick Rose, John Legend and the Chicago Sky appear alongside picturesque drone shots of the city.</p><p>“From the factory floors to the halls of power, Chicago is the birthplace of change,” a voiceover says in the video. “In the face of adversity, Chicago never backs down.”</p><p>During the 2024 convention, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2024-democratic-national-convention/2024/08/19/dnc-protest-march-on-the-dnc-union-park-monday" target="_blank" ><u>thousands protested</u></a> near the United Center, the convention’s venue, decrying the Democrats’ response to the war in Gaza and calling for the U.S. to cut ties with Israel. But the protests were largely peaceful and city officials considered the 2024 convention a success, partly due to the event generating <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2024-democratic-national-convention/2024/10/17/democratic-national-convention-chicago-economic-impact" target="_blank" ><u>$317 million for the local economy</u></a>.</p>
entries.author: Mary Norkol

entries.title: Chicago No. 1 in annual ranking for corporate relocations
entries.summary: <p>The Chicago area was named the top U.S. metro for corporate relocations and site selection for the 13th year in a row by Site Selection Magazine, the World Business Chicago announced on Monday.</p><p>World Business Chicago, the city’s nonprofit economic development agency, last year counted 223 corporate expansions, projects and new entrants in the Chicago area. The 40% year-over-year increase in activity represents $1.7 billion in annual earnings and the creation of an estimated 19,600 jobs.</p><p>The projects include office, industrial, corporate headquarters, warehouse, data and call centers. Some launched in 2025, while others will open in 2026, 2027 and beyond.</p><p>The Chicago area’s “industrial depth, combined with modern infrastructure and a skilled workforce, is why companies continue to choose Chicago to grow, invest and innovate,” Mayor Brandon Johnson said in a news release.</p><p>He cited recent projects like Universal Horror Unleashed, Hexaware Technologies, The Hand and The Eye, Freedman Seating Co., Infleqtion and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/theater/2025/10/28/david-byrne-chicago-goodman-theatre-immersive-theater-of-the-mind" >Theatre of the Mind</a>, the collaboration between David Byrne and The Goodman Theatre.</p><p>World Business Chicago CEO <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2024/04/16/world-business-chicago-ceo-clement" >Phil Clement</a> said the organization’s long-term strategy, called Chicago 2050: A Plan for Economic Growth and Jobs, outlines a road map to grow the regional economy from about $950 billion today to more than $1.4 trillion by 2050.</p><p>Last May, Hexware Technologies opened a new office in the Loop at 145 S. Wells St. The global IT firm expects to add 250 employees over five years.</p><p>Amazon announced a fulfillment center in Bolingbrook that is expected to create some 1,200 jobs after it opens later this year.</p><p>The $185 million Hollywood Casino Joliet opened last summer.</p><p>Fortune Brands Innovations, a security and digital products company, expanded in Deerfield. It expects to create at least 400 full-time jobs by 2027.</p><p>Magic venue The Hand and The Eye will open this spring in Downtown at the historic McCormick Mansion, 100 E. Ontario St. The $50 million, 35,000-square-foot site will offer “immersive theater, dining and illusion,” according to World Business Chicago.</p><p>In River West, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/river-west/2025/06/26/universal-horror-unleashed-theme-park-ballys-casino-comcast-world-business-chicago" >Universal Horror Unleashed</a> will transform a 114,000-square-foot commercial building, formerly the Chicago Tribune’s distribution center at 700 W. Chicago Ave., into an entertainment complex. The project will create about 400 jobs and is scheduled to open next year.</p><p>Universal Destinations & Experiences, a division of Comcast NBCUniversal, chose Chicago to build its second year-round House of Horrors entertainment complex in the nation — and the first in the Midwest. The first park opened last year in Las Vegas.</p><p>Of more than 200 projects announced last year, manufacturing remained the largest sector, accounting for 38%, according to World Business Chicago.</p><p>Professional, scientific and technical services represented 16% of projects, highlighting expansion in engineering, technology and innovation. Transportation and warehousing made up 13%. Finance and insurance accounted for 6%.</p><p>“Chicago wins because of structural advantages — central geography at the heart of North American trade, the busiest multimodal freight network in the country, unmatched global connectivity through O’Hare and a 5.5 million–person workforce that spans advanced research to essential operations,” Clement said in a news release.</p>
entries.author: Amy Yee

entries.title: Cubs' Jameson Taillon honors family history with return to Team Canada for WBC
entries.summary: <p>GOODYEAR, Ariz. — At 8 years old, Jameson Taillon wasn’t exactly thrilled to be trapped at the table by nagymama’s (which is Hungarian for grandmother) tales of World War II. She talked about it all the time, and the boy’s understanding of the world wasn’t developed enough to appreciate what his grandparents went through to escape Hungary.</p><p>Now, Taillon cherishes his memories of those conversations with his grandmother, Agnes Kormendy, which she also wrote down in a book. In fact, that family history is the emotional core of Taillon’s commitment to Team Canada.</p><p>“Canada just welcomed her,” Taillon said in a recent conversation with the Sun-Times. “She built an incredible life and family in Canada.”</p><p>On Monday, Taillon made his last spring training start with the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a> before joining the Canadian national team in Puerto Rico for the World Baseball Classic. He’s expected to make his first start of the tournament on Sunday against Panama.</p><p>“I'm doing it more to honor my family and what Canada means to my family, and how great it's been to us,” he said. “I still want to win and stuff, but I think helping Canada grow baseball is also a big deal.”</p><p>Taillon grew up in Texas, but his family ties to the Great White North are extensive. Both his parents were born and raised in Canada, he said. And Taillon’s parents made sure every summer that their four kids visited their grandparents in Canada.</p><p>Taillon’s brother Jordan was born not far from Toronto. And the eldest, Justin, returned to get his master’s degree from the University of Guelph in Ontario. They still have plenty of family and friends in the country.</p><p>This will be the second time Taillon represents Canada in the WBC. The first was in 2013, when Taillon was a 21-year-old Pirates prospect, just a few years removed from being selected No. 2 overall by Pittsburgh in the MLB Draft.</p><p>“Being a prospect at the time, I was just handled carefully in a lot of ways,” he said, “protected a little bit.”</p><p>Then for the WBC, he ventured from the Pirates organization, onto the international stage, and into another world. Taillon, who had never pitched above Double-A at that point in his career, was tabbed to start against Team USA in pool play.</p><p>He was facing a lineup that had Jimmy Rollins, Brandon Phillips, Ryan Braun and Joe Mauer hitting back-to-back-to-back-to-back. Then after getting through them, he’d still had to worry about David Wright, Adam Jones and Ben Zobrist in the bottom half of the order.</p><p>“That was the big thing for me, was putting my big boy pants on and going out there,” Taillon said. “I was scared as s—. But you’re out there facing [future Hall of Famer] Joe Mauer, who’s coming off MVPs, and Ryan Braun, and it’s like, well, you have no choice but to just try to do your best.”</p><p>Young Taillon’s best was pretty darn good. He held the U.S. to just one earned run and four hits in four innings. The game was tied when he handed over the ball, but the United States went on to beat Canada 9-4.</p><p>Taillon’s return to the WBC comes at a wildly different point in his career. Far from a burgeoning prospect, the 34-year-old is entering his 10th major-league season. He’s played for three different franchises, come back from cancer and two separate Tommy John surgeries, and continued to evolve as a pitcher.</p><p>He joins Team Canada as an elder statesman, his focus on preparing for a major-league season, but his foundation solid enough to also balance a commitment to his national team.</p><p>“I’m excited,” Taillon said Monday to a small group of reporters, standing by the batting cage tucked below Goodyear Ballpark’s right-field seating. “I wish I was throwing better going into it, but honestly, I think it could be good. A little change of scenery, get out of Arizona, go compete.”</p><p></p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" data-module data-column-count=3 data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" entries.title: South Chicago residents, businesses form coalition supporting Illinois' quantum computing campus
entries.summary: <p>A coalition of about 20 businesses and residents announced Monday a new group called the Southeast Neighbors For Quantum to show their support for the 128-acre Illinois Quantum and Microelectronics Park, despite pushback from some Chicagoans over the massive development project.</p><p>Members of the coalition — ranging from fourth-generation residents of South Chicago and South Shore to small businesses and local school officials — expressed a desire to not let South Chicago’s chance for economic growth and investment pass by, during a news conference.</p><p>Jorge Perez, owner of Chico’s Oven in South Chicago, said the coalition sprang from a desire to ensure “the authentic voice of Southeast Chicago residents” is involved in the quantum park project and the entire 400-acre <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/technology/2024/07/26/pritzker-silicon-valley-quantum-development-south-works-crg-related-midwest" >Quantum Shore</a> development. The Illinois Quantum and Microelectronics Park makes up a portion of the massive Quantum Shore development, which will include a 52-bed <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2024/12/17/advocate-health-care-replace-trinity-hospital" >Advocate Health Care</a> hospital.</p><p>“This is a once in a lifetime opportunity,” Perez said. “We're actually at the beginning of a new era, and we believe it holds a lot of wonderful opportunities for us and for our families.”</p><p>Perez said he and other residents have been frustrated by quantum campus protesters, who are from other neighborhoods like Logan Square and Humboldt Park and don’t represent the feelings of South Chicago residents. The protesters also don’t support local businesses when they hold events or news conferences in the neighborhood, he said.</p><p>“We do not want — and we don't need — others from outside of the community … to speak on our behalf,” Perez said. “We’ve seen a lot of that over the last couple months.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-520000" name="image-520000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/eb716e7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2Fba%2F0be1b60d494fbf1bf2f673164a58%2F20260302-1137150.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9e6997a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2Fba%2F0be1b60d494fbf1bf2f673164a58%2F20260302-1137150.jpg data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/512195c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2Fba%2F0be1b60d494fbf1bf2f673164a58%2F20260302-1137150.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Southeast Neighbors for Quantum members Sharon Brown-Latiker (left), Jorge Perez, Erika Galan and Roni Facen" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/512195c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2Fba%2F0be1b60d494fbf1bf2f673164a58%2F20260302-1137150.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/377626b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2Fba%2F0be1b60d494fbf1bf2f673164a58%2F20260302-1137150.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/512195c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4000x2245+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F36%2Fba%2F0be1b60d494fbf1bf2f673164a58%2F20260302-1137150.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Southeast Neighbors for Quantum members Sharon Brown-Latiker (left), Jorge Perez, Erika Galan and Roni Facen</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided by Southeast Neighbors for Quantum </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Several organizations, including Southside Together and Alliance of the Southeast have held protests and voiced their opposition to the project. The alliance and its larger group, Coalition for a South Works CBA, have not necessarily opposed the quantum campus, but they've called for a community benefits agreement for any redevelopment of the former steel mill. Southside Together, which includes South Chicago residents, has strongly condemned the quantum park and has instead asked lawmakers to consider developments it thinks would better suit the community's needs, like a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2025/09/17/south-side-activists-residents-elected-officials-quantum-computing-campus" >grocery store or pharmacy</a>.</p><p>The quantum campus broke ground in October 2025 with the construction of Palo Alto, California-based <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/technology/2024/07/25/psiquantum-steel-south-works-quantum-computer-johnson-darpa" >PsiQuantum’s facility</a>. The building will house the country’s first utility-scale quantum computer.</p><p>The campus will lead to new jobs in quantum computing and related scientific fields — and more foot traffic for existing and new small businesses, Erika Galan, a South Chicago resident and program manager at the Calumet Area Industrial Commission, said.</p><p>Roni Facen, principal and CEO of St. Francis de Sales High School, said she’s already seen the impact of the quantum campus. The park is partnering with local schools to introduce quantum programming, which has helped engage students.</p><p>“We're preparing kids for jobs that don't exist yet, and I think this way they can see it. They know that it's there, and they can touch it,” Facen said. “I want to see our community change. I want to see it grow. I want to give the next generation of young people the opportunity to see that come into real play. And this is what it's about.”</p><p>Facen and Galan were involved in the creation of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2026/01/31/south-chicago-launches-new-quality-of-life-plan-ahead-of-transformational-time-for-the-neighborhood" >South Chicago Quality of Life Plan</a>, which launched in January. The plan drew feedback from more than 1,000 residents in the South Side neighborhood to identify key areas for growth over the next five to seven years. It also found the neighborhood’s population decreased by 24% since 2000, even though the area's income and education level has increased.</p><p>The new coalition sees the quantum park as a way to fight population loss and disinvestment.</p><p>Sharon Brown-Latiker, founding member of the Southeast Neighbors For Quantum and fourth-generation resident of South Chicago, has family that worked at the South Works steel mill. She said she’s excited about Quantum Shore because it will bring jobs and investments back to land that’s been vacant for years, after multiple redevelopment plans fell through.</p><p>“We have been a community that has been forgotten because we are so far over East,” Brown-Latiker said. “But I want you to know today that we will not be forgotten. South Chicago is here, and we are here to stay. Our kids and our grandkids will be inspired by this cutting-edge technology. … [What] was once an eyesore is turning into a jewel in our neighborhood.”</p>
entries.author: Abby Miller

entries.title: White Sox outfielder/DH Andrew Benintendi expects to return to action this week
entries.summary: <p>GLENDALE, Ariz. — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" >White Sox</a> outfielder/designated hitter <b>Andrew Benintendi</b> hasn’t played in a spring game since Wednesday with what he said Monday has been general soreness in his right side.</p><p>Barring any setbacks, he told the Sun-Times he expects to be back in the lineup for the Sox’ night game Thursday against the Guardians.</p><p>‘‘It’s early in camp, and we are just being cautious,’’ he said.</p><p>Manager <b>Will Venable</b> confirmed the plan before the Sox’ Cactus League game Monday against the Giants.</p><p>‘‘He’ll be ready Thursday, we think,’’ Venable said. ‘‘He was swinging today, running around today. He’d probably play if it wasn’t March 2. We’ll give him a couple of more days and get him back out there.’’</p><p>The Sox will play the Padres on Tuesday, then will enjoy a day off Wednesday. Given that the game Thursday is at night, that will give Benintendi a good portion of the week off.</p><p>He has played in only three spring games and has three hits in seven at-bats, two of them doubles.</p><p>Benintendi is coming off an injury-plagued 2025 season that began when he missed most of spring training after being hit by a pitch and suffering a non-displaced fracture in his right hand. Upon returning in April, he was sidelined by a strained left abductor. The Sox then shut him down in September because of tendinitis in his left Achilles tendon.</p><p>In all, Benintendi played in only 116 games, batting .240 with 20 home runs, 63 RBI and a 104 OPS+, just four points above the major-league average.</p><p>At 31 and going into his 11th big-league season, Benintendi senses he’s at a crossroads.</p><p>‘‘We’re not getting any younger,’’ he said. ‘‘Sometimes it’s one thing after another.’’</p><p>Asked what it has been like to be part of another Sox rebuild, he said: ‘‘We’re a couple of years into it now, so it feels less like a rebuild. Guys are getting solidified. We brought in a lot of new guys, and it will be a big year for a lot of guys coming back.’’</p><p>He’s one of them.</p><h3>Martin eyes Opening Day, leader role</h3><p>Right-hander <b>Davis Martin</b>, who started against the Giants, said he’s vying to make the Opening Day start March 26 against the Brewers in Milwaukee.</p><p>Martin, who yielded three runs and four hits, struck out two and walked two in three innings, also said he is eyeing a leadership role on the team.</p><p>‘‘Everybody is leaning into leadership,’’ Martin said. ‘‘It’s more organic than anything. I don’t think anybody has taken it upon themselves to be, ‘I’m the leader. I’m the king.’ Everybody has their areas of expertise. I still have to go in and ask questions. It’s a beautiful thing about this game: You’re never done evolving. If I’m in a leadership role, great. I’m going to be myself.’’</p><p>When he was asked about starting on Opening Day, he said: ‘‘Is it wide-open? I have no idea. We’re going to compete like it’s wide-open.’’</p><h3></h3><h3><b>Giants 6, Sox 5</b></h3><p>Sox starter<b> Davis Martin </b>allowed three runs, all in the third inning. He walked <b>Buddy Kennedy </b>and <b>Jesus Rodriguez</b> back-to-back before left fielder <b>Jarred Kelenic</b> lost a fly ball in the sun, resulting in a run-scoring double. Martin, however, took responsibility.</p><p>“Yeah, yeah, but I can’t walk the two guys in front of him,’’ he said. ‘‘I always blame myself. That’s accountability.’’</p><p>• MLB announced its Spring Breakout, which is scheduled for March 19-22 this year, will become a tournament next year. The teams will be made up of prospects from all 30 major-league clubs, with the Cactus League and Grapefruit League each crowning a champion. It will feature a single-elimination format.</p><p>• Kelenic, who is in camp as a non-roster invitee, went 2-for-3 with a double, a run scored and an RBI, but he’s still hitting only .176 for the spring. Kennedy hit the go-ahead home run for the Giants in the ninth.</p><p>• <b>On deck: </b>Padres at Sox, 2:05 p.m. Tuesday, Glendale, <b>Nick Pivetta</b> vs. <b>Sean Newcomb</b>.</p><div
