entries.title: Horoscope for Tuesday, March 03, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon Alert</h2> <p>There are no restrictions to shopping or important decisions today. The Full Moon in Virgo peaks at 5:38 a.m.</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Be patient with co-workers today as well as people who might be assisting you in health matters. Likewise, be patient with issues related to a pet because today’s full moon can trigger emotional arguments in a New York minute. Be the adult in the room.</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Despite the intense full moon energy today, this is a lovely day to socialize and enjoy the company of friends, groups and organizations. One of the reasons this is a successful day for you is because you feel positive, warm and generous to everyone. People love that.</p><h2>Gemini (May 21-June 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today you might feel caught between the demands of home and family and the demands of your external world. You will have to make a choice. Although this is a great day to entertain at home, don't ignore the demands of career and your reputation.</p><h2>Cancer (June 21-July 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Pay attention to everything you say and do because this is a mildly accident-prone day due to the full moon energy. Of course, an accident doesn't have to happen, but you could be distracted because your emotions are heightened. Be careful about arguments. You don't have to be right.</p><h2>Leo (July 23-Aug. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Financial matters are tricky today because, although you have moneymaking ideas or an opportunity to boost your income, pressure from the government, another party or your partner might be hard to deal with. Postpone these decisions for another day if you can.</p><h2>Virgo (Aug. 23-Sept. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today the full moon is in your sign, which can create a bit of angst within you. (This might not happen, but it could.) Try to avoid clashes or direct confrontations with partners, spouses and close friends, as well as members of the general public. Go gently. Pamper yourself.</p><h2>Libra (Sept. 23-Oct. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You're a people pleaser at heart and like to avoid arguments. However, today conflict might arise with co-workers, issues about pets, medical matters, or something related to hygiene and wellness. It could actually be about anything. Patience is your best friend.</p><h2>Scorpio (Oct. 23-Nov. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Do what you can to keep the peace today when dealing with your kids or romantic partners because today's full moon energy might make you come on too strong. You are the most powerful sign of the zodiac. Lighten up.</p><h2>Sagittarius (Nov. 22-Dec. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You're high-viz today, which means people notice you more than usual. However, because the full moon is at the top of your chart, it also means people will notice a public argument or any kind of strong emotion that you might usually be discreet about. Be aware of this. (Busted!)</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Steer clear of touchy, controversial subjects today because they can quickly become an argument, which will be overblown and something you might later regret. This is because today's full moon energy can blow things out of proportion. You don't need this.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Don’t make a big deal about financial issues, squabbles about responsibilities, shared property, or something that you borrowed or lent. In time, this will sort itself out, so don’t get worked up.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today's full moon is opposite your sign, creating tension with those who are closest to you, especially spouses and partners. Basically, emotions are running high, and you might say something you don't even agree with five minutes later. Stay calm for your own peace of mind.</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Actress Julie Bowen (1970) shares your birthday today. Despite your confidence and direct manner, you are deeply sensitive. You adopt a brisk, no-nonsense approach to hide your compassionate empathy. This is a year of learning and teaching. Take time to renew your spiritual and religious beliefs. You might attain a higher consciousness through meditation, prayer or introspection.</p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: Nazareth's defense intensifies late in IHSA Class 4A super-sectional win over Waubonsie Valley
entries.summary: <p>It was a bit of déjà vu for Nazareth.</p><p></p><p>The Roadrunners, once again, were on the floor at Lyons with a trip to the IHSA Class 4A state semifinals. Though the team discussed last year’s loss to Kenwood — the eventual 4A state champions — they didn’t dwell on it. The coaches addressed it once but noted that this year’s team is different from last year’s.</p><p></p><p>One separator between last year’s team and this year’s is that Nazareth is headed to the 4A state semifinals after knocking off Waubonsie Valley 54-42. The Roadrunners are advancing to Bloomington-Normal for the third time in four years. They’ll play Bellville East on Friday.</p><p></p><p>“It means the world,” said forward Stella Sakalas, who scored nine points and grabbed 11 rebounds. “I've been playing with some of these girls since fifth grade, so this is such a full-circle moment, getting to go down to Illinois State.”</p><p></p><p>Nazareth (31-3) jumped out to a 14-3 lead, but the Warriors responded in the second quarter to tie the game 25-25 at halftime. Junior guard Sophia Towne and senior guard Lyla Shelton were key in helping the Roadrunners maintain pace with Waubonsie Valley. Shelton scored eight of her game-high 18 points in the second quarter. Towne provided a three-pointer in the quarter.</p><p></p><p>Sakalas, a BYU commit, draws a lot of attention from opponents. They gear their defense towards stopping or containing her, but Nazareth isn’t offensively inept without Sakalas. The Roadrunners have multiple players who can contribute on any given night.</p><p></p><p>“Them stepping up and making these big shots is exactly what they want to do,” Sakalas said of Towne and Shelton.</p><p></p><p>In the fourth quarter, Nazareth played flawlessly. The Roadrunners executed their offense and completely disrupted the Warriors’ offensive attack with their zone defense. The Warriors scored six points in the fourth quarter and turned the ball over six times.</p><p></p><p>“Our defense just really intensified in the last quarter, which got our offense moving,” said Towne, who scored 17 points. “We really prioritized our defense in practice. Once we get those steals and fastbreaks, that's when we really make our breakthrough on offense against teams.”</p><p></p><p>Waubonsie’s 42 points were a season low. Illinois State recruit Danyella Mporokoso scored nine points, senior guard Elliana Morris added 12 points and junior guard Maya Pereda scored 12 points. Nazareth doubled Mporokoso often, ensuring she didn’t catch fire and got one of her patented scoring runs.</p><p></p><p>“Waubonsie is an incredible team, so we knew it was going to be a game, and we just figured we had to get them off the three-point line,” Sakalas said. “We stuck to our defense, and we stuck to our gameplan, and I think that's what pulled it out for us.”</p><p></p><p>Junior guard Molly Moore was disruptive at the point of attack, hounding Warriors players and creating turnovers with her ball pressure. Junior forward Sam Austin scored five points and grabbed nine rebounds. She’s one of Nazareth’s unsung heroes with her ability to impact the game without needing the ball.</p><p></p><p>The collective effort has Nazareth right where it wants to be as one of the four teams vying for a state championship in the last week of the season. The road to get there was challenging — the Roadrunners beat Young, St. Ignatius and Downers Grove North — but it prepared the Roadrunners for what will be their most important game of the season on Friday.</p><p></p><p>“This is our goal,” Sakalas said. “This is what everybody works for from that first day of practice.”<br></p>
entries.author: Kyle Williams

entries.title: Iran attack is nothing but a ploy to divert attention
entries.summary: <p>National security, my Irish-Hungarian butt. President Donald Trump’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/nation-world/2026/02/28/iran-trump-israel" >attack on Iran </a>was a brazen political stunt, meant to deflect and distract from, well, everything.</p><p>Even Pearl Harbor — the greatest premeditated attack on American soil before 9/11 — was not answered before Franklin Roosevelt went before Congress and requested a formal declaration of war on Japan.</p><p>Make no mistake; assassinating the leader of a foreign power, no matter how evil, without direct provocation, is an act of war.</p><p>With a spineless, nearly fully compliant Congress, the corrupt football toward the end zone of full authoritarianism advances another 10 yards.</p><p>Every one of us — except Sen. Lindsey Graham, R-S.C., whose shameless display of sycophancy on Sunday's "<a class="Link" href="https://www.nbcnews.com/meet-the-press/video/march-1-sen-lindsey-graham-sen-mark-kelly-and-rep-ro-khanna-258511429523" target="_blank" >Meet the Press”</a> would make his own dog run away from home — should be shaking in their boots. Impeachment No. 3, followed by removal from office, should come hard and fast. But, of course, it won’t.</p><p><i>Rob Hirsh, West Ridge</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-e20000" name="module-e20000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Give us your take</h3><br>Send letters to the editor to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. To be considered for publication, letters must include your full name, your neighborhood or hometown and a phone number for verification purposes. Letters should be a maximum of approximately 375 words.</div> </div><p></p><h3>Stop ‘madman’ </h3><p>Saturday's Sun-Times included an Associated Press <a class="Link" href="https://www.npr.org/2026/02/28/nx-s1-5730151/trump-iran-nuclear-talks" target="_blank" >story</a> that quoted Donald Trump saying of the Iranians, "I’m not happy with the fact that they're not willing to give us what we have to have. … We’ll see what happens. We’re talking later."</p><p>Hours (or maybe it was only minutes) later, reports abounded that the United States and Israel attacked Iran. </p><p>This so-called president has never had to wait for whatever he wants and doesn’t care that many <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/02/28/protesters-in-loop-condemn-u-s-attack-on-iran-war-is-not-the-solution" >Americans</a>, whom he purports to represent, don’t want any more regime-change wars. He also obviously doesn’t care about Middle East diplomacy and negotiations. He doesn’t deserve to be a dog catcher, let alone president.</p><p>It may be Israel’s and Trump’s latest trumped-up charges, or yet another of these bitter, petty men's fever dreams, but why are we again slavishly doing Benjamin Netanyahu's bidding, and why doesn't any Republican speak up for "We the People," and stop this madman?</p><p><i>Maja Ramírez, Avondale</i></p><h3>U.S. needs regime change, too </h3><p>Donald Trump is urging Iranians to take over their country for regime change. Who does he think he is? He can’t even run his own country. I urge my fellow Americans to take over and topple the regime running the United States. Let’s have a regime change here before it’s too late. Get rid of the tyrant.</p><p><i>Jackie Tinker, Des Plaines</i></p><h3>Barron can enlist </h3><p>Donald Trump speaks boldly about going to war with Iran. If he is truly confident in that decision, then perhaps the first boots on the ground should belong to his own son, Barron Trump. It is far easier to advocate for sacrifice and the inevitable loss of life when it is borne by someone else’s family.</p><p><i>Tom Scorby, St. Charles</i></p><h3>War and peace </h3><p>Attention: Oslo.</p><p>There is a new MAGA logic — the road to the Nobel Peace Prize is to attack other countries.</p><p><i>Hugh Spencer, Countryside</i> </p><h3>Man of peace?</h3><p>Isn't it interesting that Donald Trump, the man who loves to brag that he <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2026/02/25/state-union-speech-trumps-lies-immigrants-america-chicago-natalie-moore" >ended eight wars</a>, has started a shooting war with Iran?</p><p><i>Steven Herr, West Ridge</i></p><h3>Iran attack violates U.S. Constitution </h3><p>The current attack on Iran is not only an attack on Iran. It is an attack on the U.S. Constitution. The power to declare war is granted to <a class="Link" href="https://constitution.congress.gov/browse/essay/artI-S8-C11-2-1/ALDE_00000110/" target="_blank" >Congress</a>, not the president. Even after the bombing of Pearl Harbor, President Franklin Roosevelt addressed Congress and requested a formal declaration of war. </p><p>This is the second time Donald Trump has taken military action against Iran without authorization from Congress. In the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/nation-world/2025/06/21/u-s-has-struck-3-iranian-nuclear-sites-trump-says-joining-israeli-air-campaign" >June bombing</a>, Trump claimed to have obliterated the Iranian nuclear weapons program. The continued existence of that program is now part of the justification for the current war. </p><p>Trump, of course, has attacked the Constitution before when he unilaterally and without congressional authorization sought to impose tariffs. After the Supreme Court <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/economy/2026/02/20/supreme-court-trump-sweeping-tariffs-john-roberts-economic-agenda" >ruled </a>against him recently, he <a class="Link" href="https://www.democracynow.org/2026/2/23/lori_wallach_trade_tariffs" target="_blank" >attacked</a> the court. </p><p>He also attacked the Constitution in trying to mangle the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2025/01/21/birthright-citizenship-donald-trump-executive-order-14th-amendment" >14th Amendment</a> in his attempts to end birthright citizenship.</p><p>And then there's another proposed attack on the Constitution as Trump seeks to have the federal government take the power of conducting elections away from the state governments. </p><p>Voters are rightfully concerned about the economy, but even if the economy is strong, the essential action to make sure that a "government of the people, by the people, and for the people," shall not perish is to uphold the Constitution.</p><p><i>Alan Rhine, Glenview</i></p><h3>Trump followed the law </h3><p>For those who want to criticize President Donald Trump for bombing Iran, go for it, but please do not claim he is somehow violating the <a class="Link" href="https://thehill.com/video/stopping-trumps-strikes-on-iran-senate-prepares-to-vote-on-war-powers-resolution-trending/11568712/" target="_blank" >War Powers Resolution</a> or acting "unconstitutionally." He's not.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://www.congress.gov/bill/93rd-congress/house-joint-resolution/542" target="_blank" >resolution</a> was passed by Congress in 1973 over President Richard Nixon's veto. As a threshold matter, it is unclear whether the resolution itself is constitutional. Presidents — both Democrat and Republican — have argued it is not. The Supreme Court has never weighed in. Just as Congress can't pass a law outlawing the First Amendment — as that would require a separate process of amending the Constitution — the argument is Congress can't infringe on a president's commander-in-chief powers by statute either.</p><p>But even if the War Powers Resolution is constitutional, Trump has not violated it. </p><p>All it requires is that he give Congress notice of his actions within 48 hours, which he did. He then has up to 90 days to withdraw troops without congressional consent. If he wants to keep troops on the ground for more than 90 days, then he needs Congress to authorize it.</p><p>There don't appear to be any troops on the ground in Iran, but even if there are, they will likely be withdrawn well within 90 days. If not, then and only then, would Congress need to approve, assuming the War Powers Resolution is indeed constitutional.</p><p>So again, compliment or criticize the president to your heart's content, just don't claim he's violating the Constitution or law.</p><p><i>William Choslovsky, Sheffield Neighbors</i><br></p>
entries.author: Letters to the Editor

entries.title: Dear Abby: After months of couples therapy, things haven't improved
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I have been married more than 20 years to my best friend. She's the love of my life. We have been through a lot together and have been in couples counseling for eight months. We almost divorced last year because of an emotional affair I had seven years ago. (She had a similar distraction last year.)</p><p>We are friends and do everything together. I try to do everything right. I'm there for her emotionally. I have stopped drinking every day and developed a positive, mindful and kind mindset. I got myself into shape physically. I earn a good living, help around the house, prepare dinner for all of us and help with kids' appointments and activities.</p><p>The problem? My wife has physically withdrawn from me. Anything beyond hugs and kisses is too much for her. Physical intimacy happens less than once a month. I feel alone in my own home because I thrive on touch and affection but receive none. I love my wife and don't want to be with anyone else. The counselor says things "may" turn the corner "in time." In the meantime, how do I function while feeling undesired and rejected on a daily basis? — <i>FORGOTTEN HUSBAND IN THE SOUTH</i></p><p><b>DEAR HUSBAND:</b> You have my sympathy. It is possible that as much as you and your wife like and love each other, you are better friends than spouses. Because after eight months of counseling with your wife nothing has changed, there are no gestures of affection and you feel alone in your own home, it's time you found a psychotherapist of your own. It's clear that joint counseling has not been helpful.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> How do I get my daughter-in-law, "Darlene," to clean up after herself? I live in the house, pay rent and help with the bills, but she constantly creates a mess in the kitchen and everywhere else. She fills the sink with dishes daily and never washes pots and pans, to the point I can't use the kitchen to cook. Darlene doesn't work and has nothing to do all day but create a disaster and wait for me or my son (her husband) to clean up behind her.</p><p>My son and I each work full-time. He does all the laundry, cleaning and cooking. If I say anything, Darlene gets defensive and makes all kinds of excuses why she can't. (It's sheer laziness.) If I say anything to my son, he defends her because she whines and cries about how "tired" she is and claims to have all kinds of illnesses (her stomach hurts, she's on her period or just too tired). She stays up late every night and can't wake up to get my grandson to school, so my son does it every day.</p><p>I'm at my wits' end, but I don't want to create an environment where Darlene will ignore me and turn my son against me. Help! — <i>OUT OF BALANCE IN THE SOUTH</i></p><p><b>DEAR OUT OF BALANCE:</b> You cannot change the unhealthy dynamic in your son's household unless he and his wife agree to do so. From what you have written, that isn't likely to happen. Be glad that you are fully employed, because the healthiest situation for you would be to make other living arrangements.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>To order "How to Write Letters for All Occasions," send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby — Letter Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: Rev. Jackson's work to bring together working-class people of all backgrounds must continue
entries.summary: <p>Across the country — from South Carolina to the South Side of Chicago — we are celebrating the life of the Rev. Jesse Jackson. This weekend, he will finally be laid to rest.</p><p>As we funeralize Jackson and reflect on what he gave us, I find myself thinking not only about his courage, but about his strategy — and about the power of words to build or break coalitions.</p><p>On Saturday, I will stand with my son — who carries Jackson’s last name as his first. We named him Jackson in no small part because of the reverend’s importance to our people and to my own life. There were family reasons too. But it was his insistence that we build something broader than grievance — that we unite across lines others tried to harden — that made that name feel like a blessing to pass on.</p><p>It was his 1988 campaign that first pulled me into organizing. The Rainbow Coalition wasn’t poetry. It was strategy. It was a recognition that racism is the oldest political wedge in American history — used again and again to divide working people so none of us are strong enough to demand more for our children.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-830000" name="module-830000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnist </div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Columnist</div> </div><p>People sometimes call me a bridge. They mean that I am a Black civil rights leader with a white father. They mean I grew up rooted in both working-class and middle-class communities — Black and white — learning how to sit in rooms where folks didn’t look alike or vote alike and still search for common ground.</p><p>The truth is, I was raised by bridges.</p><p>My father grew up in overwhelmingly white Maine. His father — my grandfather — was a white doctor in Portland who made house calls in the city’s small Black community when few others would. Most of his patients were white, and he also served as the factory doctor in Biddeford. But there was a current in that family: serve your fellow human beings. Recognize common need. Be part of common solutions.</p><p>My father joined the Civil Rights Movement in Maine more than a decade before he married my mother. He later supported Jackson’s presidential campaigns in ’84 and ’88. And for decades, he worked with thousands of men — mostly white — helping them confront trauma, break cycles of abuse and rediscover empathy and accountability.</p><p>Those men were not caricatures. Many were crushed when factories closed and industrial America hollowed out. Many struggled with addiction. Many carried untreated trauma from childhood. My father sat with their pain without denying the systems that shaped them.</p><p>My mother was a bridge of a different kind — Black, Southern and fearless. Born into a family with generations active in the NAACP, she joined a lawsuit at 12 to integrate her local all-white girls’ high school. She spent summers helping desegregate churches in Petersburg, Virginia. Later, she co-authored<a class="Link" href="https://combineddestinies.com/" target="_blank" > “Combined Destinies: Whites Sharing Grief about Racism,”</a> exploring how racism against Black people has also wounded white people in their own lived lives.</p><p>She taught me that racism distorts everyone it touches — though not equally.</p><h3>‘White advantage’</h3><h3></h3><p>So when my white dad says we should use the phrase “white advantage” instead of “white privilege,” I listen.</p><p>Because he has seen what words can do in a room.</p><p>Say “white privilege,” and too often you trigger a litany: “My grandfather worked in a mill.” “My mother cleaned houses.” “We were poor.” In a nation built literally by the underdogs of multiple nations — and by the underdogs within our own — people hear that word and feel their family’s suffering erased.</p><p>That’s not usually what people mean when they say it. But it is often what many hear.</p><p>“White advantage,” by contrast, is harder to argue with. It does not deny hardship. It does not pretend white communities have been spared deindustrialization, opioid addiction or rising suicide rates. It simply names a measurable tilt in the system.</p><p>﻿Researchers have shown that identical resumes get different responses depending on the name at the top. That white applicants with criminal records can receive more callbacks than Black applicants with clean ones. That even when family income is similar, outcomes diverge.</p><p>That’s not about whether your life was easy. It’s about how the system sorts us.</p><p> Jackson understood this better than anyone. The Rainbow Coalition was never about denying white suffering. It was about refusing to let racism weaponize it. Racism keeps working communities weak by keeping us divided across what has historically been the most powerful line of division in this country — race — preventing working people, from union halls to churches, from our big cities to our small towns, from coming together in the interest of all our children.</p><p>If a word shuts down conversation before it begins, we should be wise enough to reconsider it — not to soften the truth, but to widen the coalition that can act on it.</p><p>As we prepare to lay Rev. Jackson to rest, I’m asking how we continue his work. How do we tell the truth about racism’s permanence and pervasiveness while still building the broad alliances necessary to defeat it?</p><p>Maybe part of the answer is simple: choose words that open doors.</p><p>Racism is real. It punishes. It persists. But if we are serious about breaking its power, we need language that allows people to see themselves in the solution.</p><p>Jackson taught us that working people can come together across lines that once seemed immovable — Black and white, North and South.</p><p>As we honor his life this week, let’s recommit to that project — with clarity, courage and words that build the coalition our children deserve.</p><p><i>Ben Jealous is a professor of practice at the University of Pennsylvania and former president and CEO of the NAACP.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-af0000" name="module-af0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Ben Jealous

entries.title: Rev. Jesse Jackson offered powerful support to Asian Americans
entries.summary: <p>When harassment and violence against Asian Americans surged during the COVID-19 pandemic, the Rev. Jesse Jackson condemned the heinous acts early on. He was outspoken at a time when many people didn’t realize or believe that anti-Asian racism was real or could happen.</p><p>Jackson wrote several Sun-Times columns in solidarity with Asian Americans. In <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2021/3/8/22320054/asian-american-hate-violence-racism-jesse-jackson" target="_blank" >March 2021</a>, he wrote that violence targeting them was “stoked to no small degree by more than a year of Trump obsessively describing the coronavirus as the ‘China Virus’ and ‘Kung Flu.’” </p><p>Asians in the U.S. were spat on and threatened, including my elderly mother on her way to work. Jackson said, “The scapegoating of Asian Americans is taking an ugly, violent turn.”</p><p>Weeks later, Jackson <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/columnists/2021/3/22/22345129/diversity-americas-strength-hateful-reaction-weakness-jesse-jackson" >wrote</a> about the murder of eight people near Atlanta, six of them Asian women ages 44 to 74.</p><p>Videos of Asians being brutally attacked and murdered underscored that <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/metro-state/2021/3/27/22354397/chinatown-rally-stop-aaip-hate-march" target="_blank" >anti-Asian hate</a> was real and dangerous. They were stabbed on the street in San Francisco; kicked in the stomach on their way to church in New York; pummeled more than 100 times in an apartment vestibule; and head-stomped to death.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-9b0000" name="module-9b0000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnists bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Columnists</h2><br><i>In-depth political coverage, sports analysis, entertainment reviews and cultural commentary.</i><br></div> </div><p>Jackson died Feb. 17 at age 84. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2026/02/17/what-we-know-rev-jesse-jacksons-death-services" >Services </a>were held Monday in South Carolina, where he was born and raised until he was 15. A “People’s Celebration” in Chicago will be held at 10 a.m. Friday, at House of Hope, 752 E. 114th St.</p><p>For those who know Jackson, it was unsurprising that he spoke up against anti-Asian hate. It was in keeping with the mission of his nonprofit Rainbow Coalition, which dates back to the 1970s and unites people for social justice. It represents the marginalized: Black Americans, Latinos, Asian Americans, Native Americans, Arab Americans, gays and lesbians, poor people, family farmers, workers and more.</p><h3>‘Jesse Jackson saw us’</h3><p>Community leaders remember Jackson’s support of the Asian American community dating back decades. In 1982, Vincent Chin was bludgeoned to death by two men with a baseball bat in Detroit after his bachelor party. There were witnesses, yet Chin’s murderers never served any prison time.</p><p>In 1984, Jackson rallied in<b> </b>San Francisco’s Chinatown alongside Vincent’s grieving mother, Lily, an immigrant from China, who fought for justice for her son.</p><p>“He brought national attention to the Vincent Chin case at a time when major news outlets were writing it off as a local matter,” activist Eddie Wong told the Sun-Times. “In a word, Jesse Jackson saw us.” Wong was the national field director of Jackson’s 1988 presidential campaign in Chicago.</p><p>The Rev. Norman Fong, a community leader and pastor at Presbyterian Church in San Francisco’s Chinatown, told the Sun-Times: “When Rev. Jackson shared the stage with Vincent Chin's mom — it was a moment of solidarity that brought tears to my eyes. Chinatown felt so uplifted with his solidarity. It was so powerful!”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/Z3vdBbRrlr8?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Jesse Jackson at Vincent Chin rally"></iframe> </div> </div></div><p>Afterward, Jackson’s speech at a Chinatown rally “was of course covered by all the press like never before. It was a ‘rainbow moment in action,” recalled Fong. His support “showed Black-Asian solidarity at the perfect moment.” Jackson compared Chin’s murder with the lynching of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2025/08/22/emmett-till-records-archive-history-black" target="_blank" >Emmett Till</a> that helped spark the Civil Rights Movement.</p><p>Jackson “is a hero to me,” said Fong, a Rainbow Coalition member. “He taught me to really appreciate what he called ‘solidarity in the struggle.’ "</p><p>Activist <a class="Link" href="https://asamnews.com/2026/02/19/love-letter-to-rev-jesse-jackson/" target="_blank" >Mabel Teng</a> was also at Jackson’s 1984 appearance in San Francisco. “It was the first time the Chinese community was embraced by a civil rights leader, embraced by yourself, and it made a significant and everlasting impression,” Teng told Jackson in a 2020 Zoom call transcribed in the digital magazine <a class="Link" href="https://eastwindezine.com/asian-americans-and-jesse-jackson-from-vincent-chin-to-the-rainbow-coalition-a-conversation-with-the-rev-jesse-jackson/" target="_blank" >East Wind</a>. “When the community was in need, you were the one to step into the situation.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-floatRight> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-8d0000" name="image-8d0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9002401/2147483647/strip/true/crop/360x202+0+79/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F55%2F9f%2F9c57e8a04385a5fc51bc5f815452%2Fjackson-wong-chicago-museum-360.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6ff240a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/360x202+0+79/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F55%2F9f%2F9c57e8a04385a5fc51bc5f815452%2Fjackson-wong-chicago-museum-360.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/569e976/2147483647/strip/true/crop/360x202+0+79/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F55%2F9f%2F9c57e8a04385a5fc51bc5f815452%2Fjackson-wong-chicago-museum-360.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Jesse Jackson, wearing a dress shirt, slacks and blazer, is seated and a woman and man stand at his side. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/569e976/2147483647/strip/true/crop/360x202+0+79/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F55%2F9f%2F9c57e8a04385a5fc51bc5f815452%2Fjackson-wong-chicago-museum-360.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0f446ad/2147483647/strip/true/crop/360x202+0+79/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F55%2F9f%2F9c57e8a04385a5fc51bc5f815452%2Fjackson-wong-chicago-museum-360.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/569e976/2147483647/strip/true/crop/360x202+0+79/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F55%2F9f%2F9c57e8a04385a5fc51bc5f815452%2Fjackson-wong-chicago-museum-360.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Eddie Wong, national field director of Jesse Jackson’s 1988 presidential campaign, and his daughter, Maggie Wong, pose with Jackson at the Chicago Chinese American Museum in February 2023.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Jackson inspired Teng to run for elected office in San Francisco. Over the years, Jackson encouraged Asian American and Pacific Islander representation in government and leadership roles.</p><p>In the Sun-Times, he also spotlighted history that many people are unaware of, since Asian Americans are rarely included in U.S. education curricula. Jackson called for reparations for Japanese Americans; 120,000 of them were imprisoned by the U.S. government during World War II. He cited the 1882 Chinese Exclusion Act that made it illegal for Chinese to immigrate to the U.S. And Jackson highlighted the largely forgotten Rock Springs Massacre of 1885 when “white mobs in Wyoming murdered 28 Chinese coal miners and burned Chinatown to the ground.”</p><p>More of that history will be learned today; Jackson pushed to include Asian American history in school curricula in New York and elsewhere. In 2021, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2021/7/9/22570236/asian-american-history-illinois-public-school-curriculum-elementary-high-school-students" target="_blank" >Illinois</a> became the first state to pass legislation requiring that schools teach Asian American history.</p><p>In his landmark 1984 appearance in San Francisco with Lily Chin and the Asian American community, Jackson said, “Those who live — we must redefine America so everybody knows everybody fits in the rainbow somewhere.” Indeed, as Jackson is laid to rest in Chicago this week, those who live must continue that mission.</p><p><i>Amy Yee is a reporter for the Sun-Times. She is the author of “</i><a class="Link" href="https://uncpress.org/book/9781469675510/far-from-the-rooftop-of-the-world/" target="_blank" ><i>Far From the Rooftop of the World</i></a><i>: Travels among Tibetan Refugees on Four Continents,” with a foreword by the Dalai Lama.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-9c0000" name="module-9c0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Amy Yee

entries.title: Collaboraction Theatre opens new House of Belonging in arts corridor along 606
entries.summary: <p>A performing arts theater is the latest addition to what arts leaders say is an increasingly blooming arts scene along <a class="Link" href="https://www.the606.org/wp-content/uploads/2015/05/The606_printable_map.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>The 606</u></a> in Humboldt Park.</p><p>Collaboraction, a social justice-oriented theater company established in 1996, opened its new 4,000-square-foot “House of Belonging” to the public this month at the former site of dog food and apparel store For Dog’s Sake.</p><p>The theater’s inaugural show in its new venue is <a class="Link" href="https://www.collaboraction.org/trial-in-the-delta-2026" target="_blank" ><u>“Trial in the Delta: The Murder of Emmett Till,”</u></a> extended until March 15.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-af0000" name="image-af0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5460dbc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2Ff8%2Ff208798e405f86e5733f30bc85d8%2Fcollabnewspace-260225-15.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7526a16/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2Ff8%2Ff208798e405f86e5733f30bc85d8%2Fcollabnewspace-260225-15.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d3c0714/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2Ff8%2Ff208798e405f86e5733f30bc85d8%2Fcollabnewspace-260225-15.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Audience members mingle before “Trial in the Delta: the Murder of Emmett Till” tat Collaboraction’s new space, which is inside Kimball Arts Center in Humboldt Park." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d3c0714/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2Ff8%2Ff208798e405f86e5733f30bc85d8%2Fcollabnewspace-260225-15.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d8ff580/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2Ff8%2Ff208798e405f86e5733f30bc85d8%2Fcollabnewspace-260225-15.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d3c0714/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff0%2Ff8%2Ff208798e405f86e5733f30bc85d8%2Fcollabnewspace-260225-15.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Audience members mingle before “Trial in the Delta: the Murder of Emmett Till” at Collaboraction’s new space, which is inside Kimball Arts Center in Humboldt Park.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The extension is a positive early sign for the theater group, which is a key tenant of the Kimball Arts Center. Collaboraction Theatre signed a 20-year lease with the building’s owner, Stocking Urban Development.</p><p>The new theater is opening at a particularly risky time for the arts. A year into President Donald Trump’s second term, his administration has ordered ideological audits of cultural institutions like the Smithsonian. Some grants awarded through <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/arts-culture/2025/05/06/nea-grants-trump-chicago-cultural-events" target="_blank" ><u>the National Endowment for the Arts</u></a> have been canceled.</p><p>In Illinois, Gov. JB Pritzker’s proposed state budget leaves <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/arts-culture/2026/02/23/pritzker-budget-arts-funding-flat-illinois-2026-2027-grants-trump" target="_blank" ><u>funding flat </u></a>for the sector in the next fiscal year. Meanwhile, a January report by a coalition of independent venue owners said many of its members are <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2026/02/03/chicago-indie-venues-health-metro-ramova-salt-shed" target="_blank" ><u>struggling to keep their lights on</u></a> due to rising costs and changing audience behavior.</p><p>Collaboraction leaders are optimistic that the House of Belonging will draw audiences, especially in a post-COVID-19 lockdown world where people are seeking meaningful human connection, said artistic director Anthony Moseley. </p><p>“People are now working at home. They watch their movies at home. This is a space to be with other people and experience art that is authentic and rooted in our belonging to one another,” he said.</p><p>The project cost between $800,000 and $1 million, said Collaboraction CEO Darlene Jackson, also known as Chicago house music queen <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/djladyd/?hl=en" target="_blank" ><u>DJ Lady D</u></a>. Significant donations and in-kind contributions from local arts foundations and organizations such as Illinois Humanities and the Chicago Community Trust helped underwrite the costs.</p><div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Darlene Jackson, executive director and CEO of Collaboraction, is a popular and widely respected house music DJ who performs as Lady D.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Morgan Ciocca/Vocalo</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In addition to the new 99-seat black box theater, the space includes a 49-seat lounge with a full-service bar and a small stage for variety shows, live-band karaoke, pre- and post-show community conversations and youth programming.</p><p>Jackson and Moseley are betting, too, that the building itself will become a destination. <a class="Link" href="https://kimballartschicago.com/tenants" target="_blank" ><u>The Kimball Arts Center already houses</u></a> tattoo shops, art studios, a coffee importer, a spa, a dog chew manufacturer, therapists’ offices and a salon. The building is home to artsy cafe Dayglow Coffee, too.</p><p>The theater’s presence has been received positively by other KAC tenants and ownership, Jackson said.</p><p>Foot traffic to the arts center is looking promising, especially with proximity to The 606, also known as the Bloomingdale Trail. The 2.7-mile elevated trail runs east to west along Bloomingdale Avenue before ending at the <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagoparkdistrict.com/parks-facilities/exelon-observatory" target="_blank" ><u>Exelon Observatory</u></a>.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-570000" name="image-570000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b2c9ce8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F16%2F83%2F21728e6148ba9d51a1c9cb151645%2Fcollabnewspace-260225-27.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e27e649/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F16%2F83%2F21728e6148ba9d51a1c9cb151645%2Fcollabnewspace-260225-27.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fa714ae/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F16%2F83%2F21728e6148ba9d51a1c9cb151645%2Fcollabnewspace-260225-27.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="The Kimball Arts Center is located at 1757 N. Kimball Ave. in Humboldt Park." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fa714ae/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F16%2F83%2F21728e6148ba9d51a1c9cb151645%2Fcollabnewspace-260225-27.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1f364d9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F16%2F83%2F21728e6148ba9d51a1c9cb151645%2Fcollabnewspace-260225-27.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fa714ae/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7008x3933+0+369/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F16%2F83%2F21728e6148ba9d51a1c9cb151645%2Fcollabnewspace-260225-27.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Kimball Arts Center is located at 1757 N. Kimball Ave. in Humboldt Park.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>New energy is flowing into the building, with the majority of the theater’s programming happening outside of normal working hours on evenings and weekends. And when the theater hosts matinee shows, attendees are “exploring the building” and shopping with other tenants, Jackson said.</p><p>Previously, Collaboraction operated out of the Flatiron Arts Building in Wicker Park.</p><p>But Humboldt Park’s House of Belonging is helping usher in “a new generation, a new decade for Collaboration. It’s a new era,” Jackson said, especially as the organization celebrates its 30th anniversary this year.</p><p>As a community-centered organization, Collaboraction’s mission goes beyond platforming its own work. With actor and playwright Sandra Delgado at the helm, the group launched its “You Belong Here” series.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Kimball Arts Center, located at 1757 N. Kimball Ave. in Humboldt Park, is seen in this photo, Thursday, Feb. 19, 2026. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The House of Belonging program highlights hyperlocal talent through one-night events and a few anchor programs. As part of the deal, the artists benefit from not just a place to perform, but they get technology, marketing and production support, Jackson said.</p><p>A regular anchor event of “You Belong Here” is Rudy Mendoza’s Spanish-language <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/bilingualimprovschool/?hl=en" target="_blank" ><u>Bilingual Improv School</u></a>, where all levels of language fluency and improv experience are welcome to attend drop-in classes or enroll in the eight-week program.</p><p>And in February, Collaboraction hosted a comedy and music jam with multi-hyphenates <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/binkey1/?hl=en" target="_blank" ><u>Binkey</u></a> <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/thatsmaxthomas/?hl=en" target="_blank" ><u>& Max</u></a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/windyindiemusic/?hl=en" target="_blank" ><u>violinist Windy Indie</u></a> as part of a Friday night showcase.</p><p>“We really want to support and be a space for artists in the area,” Jackson said. “You don't have to go Downtown or to the Far North Side … It could happen for you in your community.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> entries.title: Illinois' prison health care failures
entries.summary: <h3>Good morning, Chicago. ✶</h3><p>🔎 <b>Below</b>: The Illinois Department of Corrections has been under a consent decree since 2019, but the state continues to fail to provide adequate medical and dental care to incarcerated people.</p><p>🗞️ <b>Plus</b>: A cop faces firing for shooting a 13-year-old in 2022, the late Rev. Jesse Jackson's South Carolina homecoming and more news you need to know.</p><p>📧 <b>Subscribe</b>: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletter-hub.suntimes.com/" target="_blank" >Get this newsletter delivered to your inbox weekday mornings.</a></p><p>⏱️: <i>An 8-minute read</i></p><hr><p></p><h4>TODAY’S WEATHER ☁️</h4><p>Mostly cloudy with a chance of rain and a high near href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjddkkt-l-t/" target="_blank" >Illinois prisons, ordered to improve health care for inmates, have spent 7 years failing</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjddkkt-l-i/" target="_blank" >Kaitlin Washburn</a></h5><p><b>Full-blown crisis</b>: In Illinois prisons, the state's critically understaffed facilities struggle to deliver proper medical and dental care to the tens of thousands of inmates in their care, according to a court-appointed monitor. That’s despite the Illinois Department of Corrections being under a court-enforceable settlement agreement since 2019 to improve health care. </p><p><b>Key context</b>: People in American prisons have a constitutional right to adequate medical care. Not providing it to them is considered cruel and unusual punishment under the Eighth Amendment — the basis for the class action lawsuit against IDOC that triggered the consent decree.</p><p><b>One man's story</b>: Johnnie Flournoy, 74, is incarcerated at Pinckneyville Correctional Center, a medium security prison. He's been in Illinois prisons since the early 1990s and has struggled to receive consistent medical treatment for his severe glaucoma, resulting in blindness in his left eye and degenerating vision in the right. "They let me go blind," he told the Sun-Times, "and they still ain’t doing nothing."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjddkkt-l-d/" target="_blank" ><b>READ >Chicago police officer faces firing for shooting 13-year-old</a></h3><h3></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjddkkt-l-k/" target="_blank" >Sophie Sherry</a></h5><p><b>Cop faces firing</b>: Chicago Police Officer Noah Ball faces dismissal nearly four years after he shot and paralyzed a 13-year-old boy who was holding a cellphone that was mistaken for a gun. The boy was paralyzed from the waist down, according to his family’s attorney.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: It’s the second time the Civilian Office of Police Accountability has pushed to fire Ball over an on-duty shooting. In the earlier case, Ball was given a one-day suspension after Supt. Larry Snelling fought COPA’s recommendation while the second investigation was well underway.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjddkkt-l-u/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>REV. Jackson’s grand homecoming to South Carolina</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjddkkt-l-n/" target="_blank" >Lauren FitzPatrick</a></h5><p><b>Rev. returns</b>: For most of his 84 years, the Rev. Jesse Jackson powered through so many high places — organizing boycotts, freeing hostages and launching two presidential bids — that it begs remembering he wasn’t born a giant. And despite living most of his life in Chicago, that is not where his story began. Jackson died Feb. 17 and lay in honor Monday in the capitol rotunda of South Carolina, the state he thought of as home and to which returned throughout his life.</p><p><b>Son of South Carolina</b>: Before he landed in Chicago in his 20s to establish his own power, starting out with Dr. Martin Luther King Jr., activism was something Jackson had to learn from those around him. These folks include mentors and teachers who nurtured and bolstered him and his classmates from Sterling High School, once an institution for Greenville, his hometown.</p><p><b>Our deep dive</b>: Sun-Times and WBEZ reporters flew to South Carolina to attend Jackson's memorial services, trace his early steps, meet his peers, and learn more about the places that nurtured and inspired the Civil Rights trailblazer.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjddkkt-l-x/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>MORE NEWS YOU NEED 🗞️</h4><div The university confirmed last week it planned to shut the museum permanently June 30.</li></ul><hr><p></p><h4>LET’S HEAR FROM YOU 🗣️</h4><p>Have you had a medical procedure recently at a Chicago-area hospital? Were you surprised by the bill?</p><p>If so, <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjddkkt-l-w/" target="_blank" >Sun-Times consumer reporter Stephanie Zimmermann</a> wants to hear from you.</p><p>Her ongoing investigation, in cooperation with the Mansueto Institute for Urban Innovation at the University of Chicago, is finding that prices for common procedures and diagnostic tests can vary widely depending on your health insurance plan and which hospital you use.</p><p>People with high-deductible plans are the most likely to notice high hospital prices. That's especially true at the beginning of the calendar year.</p><p>Tell Stephanie your story by emailing her at <a class="Link" href="mailto:szimmermann@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >szimmermann@suntimes.com</a> or by <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjddkkt-l-yd/" target="_blank" >filling out this simple form</a>. She may contact you for a future story. State St. in the Loop.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjddkkt-l-yk/" target="_blank" >Voters turn out across Chicago’s 50 wards and suburbs ahead of March 17 primary</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjddkkt-l-yu/" target="_blank" >Elleiana Green</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjddkkt-l-jl/" target="_blank" >Violet Miller</a></h5><p>Early voting underway: Early voting locations in all 50 wards, and in 55 places in suburban Cook County, have opened ahead of the March 17 primary election. Additional early voting sites are also open in the collar counties. </p><p>At the polls: The Sun-Times went to several polling places Monday to talk with voters about the issues on their minds, including: Affordability, health care and employment, skepticism about mail-in voting, and campaign money sources.</p><p>Voter resources: <br></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dd69522/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5911x3317+0+313/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7e%2F4e%2Ff2f3678f4b6f9571fcdcaf702f85%2Fblackfilmclub-260223-001.jpg"
