alt="Black Film Club Collective co-founders Troy Martin (left) and Chinyere "Chi" Achebe sit in a theater at Gene Siskel Film Center in the Loop on Thursday, Feb. 12, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dd69522/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5911x3317+0+313/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7e%2F4e%2Ff2f3678f4b6f9571fcdcaf702f85%2Fblackfilmclub-260223-001.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9beec32/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5911x3317+0+313/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7e%2F4e%2Ff2f3678f4b6f9571fcdcaf702f85%2Fblackfilmclub-260223-001.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dd69522/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5911x3317+0+313/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7e%2F4e%2Ff2f3678f4b6f9571fcdcaf702f85%2Fblackfilmclub-260223-001.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Troy Martin and Chinyere Achebe co-founded the Black Film Club Collective.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjddkkt-l-ty/" target="_blank" >Black Film Club Collective provides space for Chicagoans to enjoy cultural stories</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjddkkt-l-tj/" target="_blank" >Erica Thompson</a></h5><p>At a recent screening of Akinola Davies Jr.’s "My Father’s Shadow," viewers in attendance at the Gene Siskel Film Center said the film resonated with them because it reflected their lived experiences. From America to Nigeria to Cameroon, their backgrounds represented the diversity of the African diaspora. After the credits rolled, they remained in the theater to share those details, along with their thoughts about the plot, characters, dialogue and cinematography. As part of the Black Film Club Collective, they are used to such rich discussions.</p><p>Led by Chinyere Achebe and Troy Martin, the organization hosts regular viewings and other events in theaters as well as coffee shops, barber shops, art galleries and other nontraditional venues. It has also partnered with institutions to develop archival projects, present a local film symposium and curate audiences for major events, such as an early Chicago premiere of "Sinners" that included a panel featuring director Ryan Coogler and blues musician Buddy Guy.</p><p>Even as the group’s footprint continues to grow, it remains committed to its goal of building community around Black film.</p><p>Before meeting and becoming friends, Achebe and Martin were separately hosting movie meetups at their homes. They officially launched the Chicago Black Film Club Collective in 2021 with a showing of John Singleton’s “Higher Learning” at a friend’s loft. Attracting an average of 40 attendees, each event maintains an intimate and welcoming environment. Achebe and Martin also partner with local vendors to offer food and beverages.</p><p>"The film club always feels like a living room, with a recognizable feeling of comfort where people can actually feel open to express themselves and just have a good time," said Martin, 37, of East Garfield Park. "It’s made up of the cinephile as much as it is the person that casually goes to a film, but the fact that we are so focused on Black storytelling sets us apart.​​​​​​"</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjddkkt-l-tt/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h4><p><b>Yesterday, we asked you</b>: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjddkkt-l-td/" target="_blank" >What do you think would be an effective way to address violent crime on the CTA?</a></p><p>Here’s some of what you said, edited for length and clarity:</p><p>"The Purple line should run express 24/7. Trains should run more frequently. At transfer points, sync train arrivals so riders don't have long waits on platforms. The length of nonrush hour trains should be shortened, with the exception of major events like baseball games, demonstrations and mega concerts." — <i>Cynthia Kirk</i></p><p>"In addition to eliminating homeless hangouts at ends of train lines, and offering unhoused and addicted people connections to services, I'd like to see trained civilian 'CTA ambassadors' riding trains and buses, offering friendly guidance to other passengers and in cases of danger, [being] empowered to call police who could meet the train or bus at the very next stop." — <i>Bonni McKeown</i></p><p>"[In the] short term, CTA and [the Chicago Police Department] can install patrol officers. They do not have to stay in one station, but can be assigned to every other train and ride with the passengers. Long term, which I think CTA is already implementing, is to improve the quality of service, which will allow more people to use the system."— Frank Ko </p><p>"Two uniformed police officers on every train, at least on the lines with high crime rates. I don’t care what agency they represent, but they need to have actual arrest powers and serve as a visible deterrent to crime. Stop running the Red and Blue Lines 24 hours a day. Both lines should shut down for at least two hours overnight when every train can be cleared and cleaned." — <i>Tommy Hoyt</i></p><p>"[Improve] the Chatbot on the CTA's website that allows you to report issues [so that it] actually resulted in immediate action. If I'm witnessing something that seems like it might escalate to violence, I should be able to ... know that as soon as I hit 'submit' on my report, within 1-2 stops, there will be law enforcement ... coming onto the train car to de-escalate the situation." — <i>Joe Karamanski</i></p><hr><p>Thanks for reading the Sun-Times Morning Edition!<br>Got a story you think we missed? <a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >Email us here.</a></p><hr><p><b>Written and curated by</b>: Matt Moore<br><b>Editor</b>: Eydie Cubarrubia<br><b>Hat tip</b>: Sun-Times' Matt Corradino for "Goner Murphy" and "Hall of claimer," headlines you'll find in the sports section of <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjddkkt-l-th/" target="_blank" >today’s Sun-Times print edition</a>. </p><hr><p><i>The Chicago Sun-Times is a nonprofit supported by readers like you. Become a member to make stories like these free and available to everyone. Learn more at </i><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wjddkkt-l-tk/" target="_blank" ><i>suntimes.com/member</i></a><i>.</i></p>
entries.author: Matt Moore

entries.title: Man shot to death in Roseland home
entries.summary: <p>Someone fatally shot a man in a Roseland home Monday night on the Far South Side. </p><p>The man, whose age wasn’t immediately known, was found around 9:55 p.m. inside the home in the 10600 block of South Forest Avenue, Chicago police said. </p><p>He suffered gunshot wounds to the chest and abdomen and was pronounced dead at the scene, police said. </p><p>Area 2 detectives were investigating. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Se completa la construcción de las primeras casas asequibles en Back of the Yards
entries.summary: <p>El viaje de DeJuanda Fairley-Hall a su trabajo está a punto de ser mucho más corto.</p><p>Fairley-Hall se está preparando para mudarse a Back of the Yards, el mismo vecindario donde ha trabajado como consejera para las Escuelas Públicas de Chicago (CPS) durante más de 17 años.</p><p>Ella está entre varias familias que están comprando casas recién construidas en el vecindario a través de una iniciativa respaldada por la Municipalidad diseñada para hacer que la propiedad de vivienda sea más asequible.</p><p>“Podré conectarme a otro nivel con una comunidad de la que he sido parte durante tantos años”, dijo Fairley-Hall, de 45 años. “Como residente, podré generar cambios en la comunidad”.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-ca0000" name="module-ca0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Actualmente está alquilando un apartamento en el vecindario de Auburn Gresham y dice que espera darle a su hija de 11 años espacio para crecer en un hogar propio.</p><p>Fairley-Hall y otros futuros propietarios de vivienda recorrieron dos casas recién construidas disponibles para la compra el sábado durante una ceremonia de corte de listón en la cuadra 5000 al sur de Aberdeen Street.</p><p>Las casas fueron construidas en terrenos anteriormente vacantes como parte de la campaña “<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2021/6/28/22554061/north-lawndale-housing-reclaiming-communities-campaign-chicago-public-private-venture" target="_blank" >Reclaiming Chicago”</a>, liderada por la comunidad, que se lanzó en 2021. Respaldada por la Municipalidad, el estado y prestamistas privados, la iniciativa tiene como objetivo construir 2,000 casas en los lados sur y oeste.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>El alcalde Brandon Johnson y DeJuanda Fairley-Hall cortaron el sábado un listón afuera de las nuevas casas construidas en el vecindario de Back of the Yards.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Giacomo Cain/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Lizette Carretero, directora de Bienestar Financiero en el Proyecto Resurrección, dijo que se compraron 200 terrenos vacantes en Back of the Yards, con 28 casas que se esperan completar este año.</p><p>“Este es un momento de impulso y esperanza”, dijo Carretero. “Tenemos a tantos miembros de la comunidad emocionados. Esa es la energía que necesitamos para seguir adelante y luchar por más trabajo y más dinero que necesitamos para poder apoyar a más familias”.</p><p>Carretero dijo que cada casa cuesta alrededor de $500,000 para construir, pero se venderá por $315,000. Los compradores pueden calificar para recibir hasta $100,000 en asistencia para el pago inicial, junto con $50,000 adicionales en apoyo del estado.</p><p>“Queremos que las familias sepan que comprar una casa es factible”, agregó Carretero.</p><p>Carretero mencionó que el Proyecto Resurrección está trabajando actualmente con aproximadamente 300 familias para prepararlas para que sean dueños de casa. La mayoría de los participantes son familias de clase trabajadora que están en proceso de volverse elegibles para comprar una casa.</p><p>Muchos de los compradores también serán dueños de casa por primera vez y podría ser el primer paso para construir riqueza generacional para sus familias.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Los visitantes revisan el sábado las nuevas casas construidas en Back of the Yards.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Giacomo Cain/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Al comprar una de estas casas, las familias “no sólo han elegido comprar una propiedad, sino que [ellas] están comprando parte de la comunidad”, dijo Raúl Raymundo, director ejecutivo del Proyecto Resurrección.</p><p>“No solo van a contribuir a su vecindario, sino a la ciudad en su totalidad, porque ahora poseen un pedazo de la ciudad”, agregó Raymundo.</p><p>También se están construyendo casas en Chicago Lawn, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2024/11/28/city-backed-plan-reclaiming-chicago-vacant-lots-homes-results-south-west-sides" target="_blank" >North Lawndale</a>, Roseland y Washington Park.</p><p>“Al convertir estos terrenos vacantes en nuevas casas, no solo estamos aprovechando terrenos no utilizados, sino que también estamos haciendo que los vecindarios sean más seguros”, dijo el alcalde Brandon Johnson en la ceremonia.</p><p>“Estamos ampliando las oportunidades para la propiedad de vivienda asequible. Pero más importante aún, estamos dando a las personas y a las familias que llamarán a este lugar su hogar la oportunidad de crear una base sólida”.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Cindy Hernandez

entries.title: Review: Shattered Globe’s ‘Morning, Noon and Night’ packs in COVID-19 lessons
entries.summary: <p>In the height of the pandemic, when we were all on lockdown, a bunch of us got sucked into new and sometimes wonky hobbies.</p><p>I have friends who learned to bake, studied music virtually and one who started collecting plants. I briefly got sucked into the world of Marie Kondo, a Japanese woman who made videos on her method of tidying-up our homes.</p><p>I never imagined what it would have been like to have Kondo in my house literally — but a similar circumstance gets imagined in “Morning, Noon, and Night,” making its Chicago premiere at Theater Wit in a <a class="Link" href="https://www.theaterwit.org/tickets/productions/561/performances#top" target="_blank" ><u>production by Shattered Globe</u></a>.</p><div class="RatingCard Enhancement" data-module data-rating="3.0" data-align-center> <div class="RatingCard-title"> “Morning, Noon and Night” </div> <div class="RatingCard-content"> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> </div> </div><p></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-090000" name="module-090000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><b>When</b>: Through March 28<br><b>Where</b>: Theater Wit. 1229 W Belmont Ave. <br><b>Info</b>: Tickets from $20</div> </div><p>Written by Obie Award–winning playwright Kirsten Greenidge, “Morning, Noon, and Night,” isn’t the first play I’ve seen this year set in the shadows of the COVID-19 pandemic (“<a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/theater-stages/2026/02/23/definition-theatre-black-cypress-bayou-review-family-chicago-stages" target="_blank" ><u>Black Cypress Bayou</u></a>,” at Definition Theatre through March 15, also unfolds in this time). Here, we get the post-pandemic story of a single mom so overwhelmed with the loss of her husband, the estrangement of her eldest daughter, and the evolving dynamic of her now remote job — that she becomes lost in the literal and metaphorical clutter that has engulfed her life.</p><p>She’s struggling to embrace the “new normal.”</p><p>As someone who lived through the COVID times, I remember surgical face masks and the overuse of hand sanitizer. But these were not the only defining characteristics of post-pandemic America. This was also a time of George Floyd’s murder at the hands of Minneapolis police, and an era of mass protest, at home and abroad.</p><p>Despite its deliberate pandemic setting, and a Black mother-daughter at its heart, the play doesn’t tackle such themes. Thus the time period is reduced to overzealous health precautions and miscommunications during virtual work meetings.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" alt="Shattered Globe Theatre’s Midwest Premiere of Morning, Noon, and Night by Obie Award-winner Kirsten Greenidge is a mind-bending drama exploring teens, family, parenting, surveillance, and connection in a post-pandemic world." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/640aad6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7099x3984+0+311/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc9%2F97%2Fc00f24174d1eb2215524e5d357fe%2Fsgt-morning-noon-and-night-4654-credit-michael-brosilow.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ba4b177/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7099x3984+0+311/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc9%2F97%2Fc00f24174d1eb2215524e5d357fe%2Fsgt-morning-noon-and-night-4654-credit-michael-brosilow.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/640aad6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7099x3984+0+311/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc9%2F97%2Fc00f24174d1eb2215524e5d357fe%2Fsgt-morning-noon-and-night-4654-credit-michael-brosilow.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The set by Jackie Fox does a great job of conveying a sense of voyeurism.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Michael Brosilow</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>But alas, here we are. In Boston, in what may be the most cluttered living room I’ve ever seen.</p><p>The set by Jackie Fox does a great job of conveying a sense of voyeurism. The theater walls are bare, allowing for a series of projections that let the audience see the characters’ smartphone screens. The set has a living living room on a raised platform with no walls, a conceit foreshadowing the lack of privacy for our main character.</p><p>The show is packed with dialogue about climate change and the ensuing doom just around the corner. It also wades into anti-consumerism, a keen take on post-pandemic America, since many people stayed home and ordered an absurd amount of packages from Amazon. Ultimately, Greenidge makes the case that people, and healthy relationships, are more valuable than material possessions.</p><p>And all that clutter? It’s the physical manifestation of the emotional baggage we keep with us. This production is about letting that stuff go and moving forward with the people you love.</p><p>These lessons are conveyed by a few high school kids. Dailyn (Emefa Dzodzomenyo) recognizes that her mother, Mia (Kristin E. Ellis), is having some serious problems. So she sets out to solve these issues in true Gen-Z form — with the internet. She creates an A.I. influencer named Miss Candice (Leslie Ann Sheppard) who autonomously creates social media content that promotes cleaning. She hopes those messages will inspire her mother.</p><p>The play tries to tackle a lot in 90 minutes, and there are times it feels a little inauthentic. A 15-year-old so obsessed with climate change that she believes the world will end in 12 years feels like a stretch. Especially when this precociously pessimistic kiddo is doomsday prophesying while simultaneously trying to get her mom to believe life is worth living. The two sides of this character feel contradictory.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d50000" name="image-d50000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4b5c4b0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7634x4284+0+502/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F58%2F1d84eb644cb68e048b37e9b3191c%2Fsgt-morning-noon-and-night-4421-credit-michael-brosilow.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1f1f68a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7634x4284+0+502/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F58%2F1d84eb644cb68e048b37e9b3191c%2Fsgt-morning-noon-and-night-4421-credit-michael-brosilow.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4f654cb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7634x4284+0+502/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F58%2F1d84eb644cb68e048b37e9b3191c%2Fsgt-morning-noon-and-night-4421-credit-michael-brosilow.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Miss Candice (Leslie Ann Sheppard, right) is an unsettling character who steps out of an algorithm and into the life of Mia (Kristin E. Ellis) in Shattered Globe Theatre’s Midwest Premiere of Morning, Noon, and Night." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4f654cb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7634x4284+0+502/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F58%2F1d84eb644cb68e048b37e9b3191c%2Fsgt-morning-noon-and-night-4421-credit-michael-brosilow.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1602b60/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7634x4284+0+502/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F58%2F1d84eb644cb68e048b37e9b3191c%2Fsgt-morning-noon-and-night-4421-credit-michael-brosilow.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4f654cb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7634x4284+0+502/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F58%2F1d84eb644cb68e048b37e9b3191c%2Fsgt-morning-noon-and-night-4421-credit-michael-brosilow.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The show is packed with dialogue about climate change and the ensuing doom just around the corner.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Michael Brosilow</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>But the show, directed by AmBer Montgomery, is enjoyable. Sheppard is enchanting as the Mary Poppins for grown ups, albeit as A.I. And Ellis’ performance as an overwhelmed single mom battling harsh circumstances at home and at (virtual) work are spot on.</p><p>It’s a tight 90-minutes and by the end, all of the issues are resolved in a <i>tidy</i> little bow. Almost too tidy.</p>
entries.author: Mike Davis | WBEZ

entries.title: Joffrey Ballet to be first U.S. company to premiere ‘Sleeping Beauty,’ Neumeier’s ‘Liliom’ in 2026-2027
entries.summary: <p>Chicago’s Joffrey Ballet will be the first American company to bring John Neumeier’s landmark narrative ballet “Liliom” and Christopher Wheeldon’s “The Sleeping Beauty” to the stage next season, placing the city’s top ballet company “at the forefront of dance,” the company announced Tuesday.</p><p>Joffrey’s 71st season will open Sept. 17-27 with the Chicago premiere of “Liliom,” a tragic story of love and redemption set in a fading American amusement park during the Great Depression.</p><p>Inspired by Ferenc Molnár’s 1909 play and later adapted into Richard Rodgers and Oscar Hammerstein’s 1945 musical “Carousel,” “Liliom” is set to multi-Academy Award-winning composer Michel Legrand’s score that blends classical and jazz influences.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-0c0000" name="image-0c0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/55ab889/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3500x1964+0+1468/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7d%2F34%2F7beeb1c548479f08fc386d440eb1%2Fliliom-courtesy-of-czech-national-ballet-photo-by-sergei-gherciu.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6d7777f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3500x1964+0+1468/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7d%2F34%2F7beeb1c548479f08fc386d440eb1%2Fliliom-courtesy-of-czech-national-ballet-photo-by-sergei-gherciu.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/95d0a13/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3500x1964+0+1468/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7d%2F34%2F7beeb1c548479f08fc386d440eb1%2Fliliom-courtesy-of-czech-national-ballet-photo-by-sergei-gherciu.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Liliom_Courtesy of Czech National Ballet_Photo by Sergei Gherciu.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/95d0a13/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3500x1964+0+1468/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7d%2F34%2F7beeb1c548479f08fc386d440eb1%2Fliliom-courtesy-of-czech-national-ballet-photo-by-sergei-gherciu.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4affc6f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3500x1964+0+1468/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7d%2F34%2F7beeb1c548479f08fc386d440eb1%2Fliliom-courtesy-of-czech-national-ballet-photo-by-sergei-gherciu.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/95d0a13/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3500x1964+0+1468/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7d%2F34%2F7beeb1c548479f08fc386d440eb1%2Fliliom-courtesy-of-czech-national-ballet-photo-by-sergei-gherciu.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“Liliom” will open Joffrey Ballet’s season in September.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Sergei Gherciu</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Closing out the Joffrey’s season in May 2027 will be the North American premiere of Christopher Wheeldon’s ”The Sleeping Beauty,” the classic fairy tale brought to life through Jerome Kaplan’s costume and set design and Tchaikovsky’s beloved score.</p><p>Ashley Wheater, the Mary B. Galvin artistic director, calls the North American premieres “works of rare theatrical scale.”</p><p>“We are proud to be the first American company to present the Chicago premiere of ‘Liliom,’ John Neumeier’s masterful, heartbreakingly beautiful narrative ballet rarely seen in the United States, as well as the North American premiere of Christopher Wheeldon’s ‘The Sleeping Beauty,’” Wheater said. “This season brings the full breadth of the art form on one stage, performed by a Company dancing at the highest level.”</p><p>Other highlights of the 2026-27 season include the return of Wheeldon’s Chicago-set take on the “The Nutcracker,” returning Dec. 4-27; “Notes on Love, a mixed rep program that will feature a world premiere by Winning Works alum Houston Thomas alongside works by late British choreographer Liam Scarlett and Joffrey rehearsal director Nicolas Blanc.</p><p>The season “reflects an organization operating at scale — investing in artists, pairing artistic ambition with structural strength and asserting Chicago’s role as a center for creative leadership,” President and CEO Greg Cameron said in a release.</p><p>This is the Joffrey’s sixth season at Lyric Opera, where it will remain through 2034. The Joffrey’s lease was slated to end following the 2027 season, but the ballet company and the Downtown opera house <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/dance/2025/07/23/joffrey-ballet-will-remain-at-lyric-opera-house-an-additional-seven-years" target="_blank" ><u>reached an extension last summer</u></a>.</p>
entries.author: Dorothy Hernandez

entries.title: Bears center Drew Dalman to retire
entries.summary: <p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> center Drew Dalman, who played every single snap after signing a three-year, $42 million contract a year ago, is stepping away.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2025/11/26/bears-nominate-center-drew-dalman-for-art-rooney-sportsmanship-award" target="_blank" >Dalman</a> has told the Bears he is retiring, a source confirmed to the Sun-Times.</p><p>It's a shocking move for Dalman, who is just 27 years old. He was the centerpiece, figuratively and literally, of a renewed offensive line that lifted Caleb Williams from one of the most-sacked quarterbacks of all time as a rookie to the team's single-season passing leader. Quarterback Caleb Williams named each lineman after an "Avengers" character, and Dalman was "The Hulk" because he was smart, strong and "the right guy for the job and my future — and our future — here," he said.</p><p>Williams took to social media Tuesday, posting a crying emoji in front of the word "Hulk."</p><p>Dalman is the son of Chris Dalman, the former 49ers interior lineman. He and his father both attended Palma High School in Salinas, Calif., before attending Stanford. A year ago, Dalman was the prize of the center free-agent market. In his first year with the Bears, he earned his first Pro Bowl berth.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="tl" dir="ltr">🥲 Hulk…</p>— Caleb Williams (@CALEBcsw) <a href="https://twitter.com/CALEBcsw/status/2028926318457323611?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 3, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Not far from his hometown for the Pro Bowl Games, he told the Sun-Times that the Bears had more work left to do after losing to the Rams in the playoffs.</p><p>“You have a little bit of satisfaction in that you took a step in the right direction and you’re building things the right way," he said. "You have evidence that the process works, so that’s encouraging. But results-wise, you’re not walking around super happy about having lost.”</p><p>Now the Bears have to find a new starting center, likely via free agency. Former Raven Tyler Linderbaum is considered the top center available, while Tyler Biadasz started 31 games the past two years as a member of the Commanders. </p><p>Asked about Linderbaum last week at the NFL Scouting Combine, Ravens GM Eric DeCosta said the team "made him a market-setting offer" and hope to reach a deal before the start of the league year next week. The Bears spoke to the agents of veteran centers at the Combine.</p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Braves' Jurickson Profar could get 162-game suspension for second positive drug test
entries.summary: <p>NEW YORK — Atlanta outfielder Jurickson Profar faces a 162-game suspension by Major League Baseball for a possible second failed test for a performance-enhancing drug, a person familiar with the issue told The Associated Press on Tuesday.</p><p>The person spoke on condition of anonymity because the process, first reported by ESPN, was ongoing.</p><p>Profar intends to ask the players' association to file a grievance to appeal any discipline to baseball's independent arbitrator, Martin F, Scheinman, a second person familiar with the process said, also on condition of anonymity, because no announcement had been made.</p><p>Because this would be Profar's second infraction, an appeal would take place after a suspension was announced.</p><p>An All-Star in 2024, Profar was suspended for 80 games last March 31 following a positive test for Chorionic Gonadotrophin (hCG), a hormone that helps production of testosterone. He issued a statement then saying: "I would never willingly take a banned substance, but I take full responsibility and accept MLB's decision."</p><p>His agent, Dan Lozano, did not immediately respond to a request for comment.</p><p>Profar homered in his return from suspension on July 2 and finished with a .245 average, 14 homers, 43 RBIs and a .787 OPS in 80 games. He batted .280 in 2024, when he set career highs with 24 homers, 85 RBIs and an .839 OPS.</p><p>Profar said at the start of spring training that he had sports hernia surgery in November, requiring a six-week recovery time. He has appeared in four spring training games this year, going 3 for 10 with three RBIs.</p><p>A native of Curaçao, Profar had been set to play for the Netherlands in the World Baseball Classic.</p><p>Under the suspension, he would be ineligible for the postseason.</p><p>Profar would lose his $15 million salary for this year as part of a $42 million, three-year contract through 2027. He lost half his $12 million salary in 2025 due to the initial suspension.</p><p>He would be the seventh player suspended 162 games for a second PED infraction after New York Mets pitcher Jenrry Mejia (July 2015), Cleveland outfielder Marlon Byrd (June 2016), free agent catcher Cody Stanley (July 2016), Houston pitcher Francis Martes (February 2020), Mets second baseman Robinson Canó (November 2020) and Milwaukee pitcher J.C. Mejia (September 2023).</p><p>Mejia received a lifetime ban in February 2016 after a third positive test, the only player to be given a permanent ban since drug testing with penalties started in 2004.</p><p>Four players have been suspended previously this year for positive tests, including free agent outfielder Max Kepler for 80 games under the major league program following a positive test for Epitrenbolone.</p><p>Following the offseason signing of left fielder Mike Yastrzemski to a $23 million, two-year deal, Profar had been targeted to be the Braves' primary designated hitter.</p><p>When catcher Sean Murphy returns from a hip injury, perhaps in May, 2025 NL Rookie of the Year Drake Baldwin could fill in at DH when not behind the plate.</p><p>With Yastrzemski, Michael Harris and Ronald Acuña Jr. in the outfield, Eli White could be a DH option. The Braves also are without projected starting shortstop Ha-seong Kim due to a finger injury. Mauricio Dubon, expected to serve a utility role, is scheduled to open the season as the starting shortstop.</p><p>The loss of Profar could create an opportunity for Dominic Smith, who signed a minor league deal on Feb. 17.</p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Three former presidents, Clinton, Obama and Biden, plan to attend Chicago service honoring Rev. Jesse Jackson
entries.summary: <p>Former Democratic presidents Joe Biden, Barack Obama and Bill Clinton plan to attend a Friday service honoring civil rights leader Rev. Jesse Jackson at Chicago's House of Hope.</p><p>The Jackson family had invited five presidents, including President Donald Trump and George W. Bush, to services honoring Jackson, who died at age 84 on Feb. 17. Biden, Obama and Clinton are confirmed to appear, according to a source with direct knowledge of the planning. It's unclear whether Bush or Trump are still planning to attend. </p><p>Former Vice President Kamala Harris, who visited with Jackson in January, was also invited to the service, although it's unclear whether she will attend.</p><p>The U.S. Secret Service has already conducted a security sweep in anticipation of presidents in attendance at the House of Hope. The event is likely to be the largest and most star-studded among the week’s worth of events. Dr. Charles Jenkins and Rev. James Meeks will co-officiate the public funeral service. Jennifer Hudson, Bebe Winans and Pastor Marvin Winans are scheduled to perform. Mayor Brandon Johnson and Gov. JB Pritzker will also speak. </p><p>On Monday, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/02/the-rev-jesse-jackson-greenville-south-carolina-colombia-greenville8-lying-in-state-chicago" target="_blank" >South Carolina sent Jackson off with high honors</a>, beneath the dome of its copper-domed statehouse. Draped in a flag, Jackson’s casket proceeded around the Capitol on a horse-drawn carriage, his children following on foot. </p><p>Thousands gathered at <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2026/02/26/rev-jesse-jackson-lying-in-repose-rainbow-push-chicago" target="_blank" >Rainbow PUSH headquarters last week</a> to pay their respects to Jackson. The family procession arrived after 8 a.m. As the morning wore on, hundreds were lined up. Johnson and the Rev. Al Sharpton were in attendance Thursday to pay their respects.</p><p>Relatives of Jackson have said that <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2026/02/17/what-we-know-rev-jesse-jacksons-death-services" target="_blank" >“all are welcome,” to services</a>, regardless of political party.</p><p>Jackson had been in declining health for a decade. In 2017 he announced he had been <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2017/11/17/18375473/rev-jesse-jackson-announces-parkinson-s-diagnosis" target="_blank" >diagnosed with Parkinson’s disease</a> two years earlier, but last April he revealed that it was actually misdiagnosed progressive supranuclear palsy, a rare neurological disorder. He stepped down as president of PUSH in July 2023, citing health concerns.</p><p>The family will livestream all services at JesseJacksonLegacy.com. Private homegoing services are scheduled for 10 a.m. Saturday at Rainbow PUSH. Doors open at 8 a.m. and capacity is limited.</p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: Pritzker says Clinton 'clearly mistaken' after ex-president testified governor was aboard Epstein's aircraft
entries.summary: <p>Illinois Gov. JB Pritzker emphatically denied any ties to convicted child sex offender Jeffrey Epstein on Tuesday after former President Bill Clinton told a congressional panel he flew on Epstein’s plane with Pritzker and his wife, MK Pritzker.</p><p>Clinton’s testimony before the U.S. House Oversight Committee delivered an unexpected broadside to Pritzker, who is weighing a potential 2028 presidential bid, and unleashed a partisan backlash that ricocheted throughout Republican-favoring corners of social media.</p><p>In Chicago, Pritzker said Clinton was “clearly mistaken” and told reporters that, in fact, he never had any dealings with Epstein nor rode aboard any Epstein aircraft.</p><p>“I'd never even heard of Jeffrey Epstein until obviously the scandal that broke out in 2019,” Pritzker said.</p><p>Republicans on the U.S. House Oversight committee released video Monday of Clinton’s hours-long deposition, where Clinton mentioned Pritzker’s name while testifying that Epstein and co-conspirator Ghislaine Maxwell were likely passengers on every flight he took on Epstein’s private jet.</p><p>Clinton has since retracted that part of his testimony.</p><p>In a statement posted on X, Clinton spokesperson Angel Urena said the former president was “simply giving an example of the many people he traveled with to see the Clinton Foundation’s work,” saying the governor joined the group on a 2008 trip.</p><p>“Not on Epstein’s plane. Not with Epstein. Not with Maxwell,” Urena said.</p><p>Despite Pritzker and Clinton’s swift denials, the former president’s deposition is making the rounds among conservatives online.</p><p>The Republican National Committee’s “X” account Monday evening uploaded a <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/RNCResearch/status/2028647022119551010" target="_blank" ><u>two-minute clip</u></a> of Clinton saying many of the flights he took on Epstein’s plane were on behalf of his Clinton Foundation. He then name-dropped Pritzker when a member of the congressional panel asked Clinton whom he would bring along with him.</p><p>“I think that I had my first trip for the man who’s now the governor of Illinois, JB Pritzker, and his wife,” Clinton said. “They helped me get started.”</p><p>The clip has been shared by U.S. Rep. Mary Miller, R-IL; the Illinois GOP account; and several Republican state lawmakers.</p><p>Impeached former Democratic Gov. Rod Blagojevich even weighed in,<a class="Link" href="https://x.com/realBlagojevich/status/2028832091949936795" target="_blank" ><u> posting</u></a> on his “X” account, “Ouch!... Will J.B. become the 2nd Pritzker to be “EPSTEINED?”</p><p>Pritzker, who is currently running for a historic third-term, had so far managed to steer clear of any public linkages to Epstein and Maxwell, though a cache of documents released last fall revealed <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/politics/2025/11/19/jeffrey-epstein-thomas-pritzker-emails-deeper-ties-jb-pritzker-cousin" target="_blank" ><u>deeper ties</u></a> between Pritzker’s cousin, Thomas Pritzker, and the disgraced financier.</p><p>Thomas Pritzker, the billionaire heir to the Hyatt Hotels empire, has not been accused of any criminal wrongdoing but <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/business/2026/02/17/thomas-pritzker-resigns-hyatt-hotels-jeffrey-epstein-emails" target="_blank" ><u>stepped down</u></a> as executive chairman of the hotel company’s board after his emailed interactions with Epstein were made public..</p><p>On Tuesday, Gov. Pritzker said he had traveled with Clinton, members of the Clinton Foundation and other “political types” in 2008 on a plane that his staff “tripled-checked” did not belong to Epstein, but instead was donated by Google.</p><p>“You know who palled around with both [Epstein and Maxwell]? Donald Trump did,” Pritzker said. “Donald Trump is known to have spent a lot of time with both of them, and yet he still hasn't owned up to it.”</p>
entries.author: Mawa Iqbal | WBEZ

entries.title: DHS head Noem claims she doesn't know about Marimar Martinez, Chicago woman shot 5 times by federal agent
entries.summary: <p>Marimar Martinez, the Chicago woman who survived being shot five times by Border Patrol agents last year, stood just five rows behind Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem as she was pushed by U.S. Sen. Richard Blumenthal to acknowledge the shooting was "wrong" during a testy Senate Judiciary Committee hearing Tuesday.</p><p>With Blumenthal describing the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/03/marimar-martinez-shot-border-patrol-chicago-speaks-out-after-good-pretti-deaths-i-am-their-voice" target="_blank" >October shooting that left Martinez with nerve damage</a> to her right hand, the Connecticut Democrat asked Noem whether Martinez's shooting was "wrong" and also highlighted — via large posters — images of text messages which showed Border Patrol agent <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/10/marimar-martinez-the-chicago-woman-shot-by-border-patrol-agent-expected-to-file-lawsuit-release-evidence" target="_blank" >Charles Exum bragging about the shooting</a> and allegedly writing, “I fired 5 rounds and she had 7 holes. Put that in your book boys.” </p><p>Despite comprehensive coverage of Martinez's shooting and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/11/20/operation-midway-blitz-marimar-martinez-anthony-ruiz-us-supreme-court-feds-drop-case-against-woman-shot-by-border-patrol-agent-in-brighton-park" target="_blank" >charges of impeding law enforcement that were later dropped</a> against her, Noem repeatedly claimed she didn't know about the case.</p><p>"Sir, I don't know the situation or the case," Noem said. "I'll look into it to ensure that all the procedures were followed properly."</p><p>Noem also would not provide details on whether Exum, the agent who shot Martinez, is still on the job, "carrying a gun." A U.S. Customs and Border Protection spokesperson last month told the Chicago Sun-Times Exum <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/11/evidence-shows-feds-lied-about-marimar-martinezs-shooting-in-chicago-attorney-says" target="_blank" >“was placed on administrative leave.”</a> The spokesperson did not respond to a follow-up email asking when the leave began, or if it’s still in effect. Noem said she didn't know the details, but "will find out and get that information to you." </p><p>"I think Americans would find it absolutely terrifying that that agent is still on the job, carrying a gun," Blumenthal said. "And the fact that you can't tell me that you will take him off."</p><p>Noem's testimony in Washington before Congress comes as DHS funding remains halted during a partial government shutdown — with Congress at an impasse over immigration policies. Despite the shutdown, senators largely focused on ICE enforcement in major cities and the fatal shootings of Alex Pretti and Renee Good by federal agents in Minnesota. </p><p>Martinez released a statement Tuesday saying Noem's claims of not knowing her case comes as "the evidence we released in my case demonstrates otherwise." She said the text messages prove Exum had Noem's support after the shooting — and she wants Noem to investigate Exum "for his attempted execution." </p><p>"Frankly, I think it is even worse if Secretary Noem does not know about the facts of my case," Martinez said. "She is the leader of DHS and one of her agents attempted to kill me, a U.S. citizen on the streets of Chicago. It was reported in every national news outlet for months. Does she not read the newspaper? Is she not briefed on shootings of U.S. citizens by border patrol agents? For the leader of DHS to not know about the shooting of a U.S. citizen, in our own country, by one of her agents is more alarming than her agency's refusal to investigate these unlawful actions." </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-2c0000" name="image-2c0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8034a58/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1680x943+0+515/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F34%2F74%2F0fdd6ad849ef8aee3251a30994c9%2Ftext.jpeg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f92a41f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1680x943+0+515/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F34%2F74%2F0fdd6ad849ef8aee3251a30994c9%2Ftext.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Text messages by Border Patrol agent Charles Exum after the Oct. 4 shooting of Marimar Martinez in Chicago.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>U.S. Attorney’s office</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Sen. Dick Durbin also asked Noem about the killings of Pretti and Good in Minneapolis, as well as Martinez's shooting in Chicago. In each case, DHS labeled the victims "domestic terrorists."</p><p>"We have ample video evidence and eyewitness testimony proving you are wrong. Your statements caused immeasurable pain to these families," Durbin told Noem. "Let me give you an opportunity to do the right thing. Do you retract these statements identifying these individuals domestic terrorists?"</p><p>Noem called the shootings "tragic situations" and said "we always offer our condolences to those families." The DHS head said she was "relying on reports from the ground and from agents that are there and working to be transparent." </p><p>Durbin asked Noem how dubbing them "domestic terrorists at the scene was somehow going to calm the situation."</p><p>"We are working in those situations where there's a tragic loss of life and that there is something that our agents are involved in, that we continue to deliver information," Noem said. </p><p>"Is it so hard to say you were wrong?" Durbin interjected.</p><p>"...I absolutely strive to provide factual information and we'll continue to do that," Noem said. </p><p>"And when you fail, do you admit it publicly?" Durbin asked. Noem replied, "Absolutely. We always know that there's room for improvement." </p><p>Noem was also repeatedly asked about Trump ally and former adviser Steve Bannon voicing support for Trump's push to nationalize elections, including deploying ICE officials to polling sites. </p><p>"There are no plans to have ICE officers at our polling locations," Noem said. </p>
entries.author: Tina Sfondeles

entries.title: People's Gas holds firm on proposed $202M price hike at City Hall hearing
entries.summary: <p>Consumer advocates urged City Council members on Tuesday to symbolically resist a proposed $202 million Peoples Gas rate hike, arguing that Chicagoans are already plagued by heating bill debt.</p><p>Although City Hall has no say in the matter, it didn't stop advocates from blasting the gas company during its first appearance before the Chicago Committee on Environmental Protection and Energy.</p><p>It will be up to the Illinois Commerce Commission to rule later this on the gas company's request to raise gas rates by an average of around $11 a month per customer, or about $130 per year.</p><p>The potential hike comes two years after the Illinois Commerce Commission granted the utility company a record $306 million rate increase. Last year regulators ordered the utility to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/consumer-affairs/2023/11/16/23963198/peoples-gas-rate-hike-pipeline-replacement-program-illinois-commerce-commission" target="_blank" ><u>overhaul its controversial pipeline replacement program</u></a> that spent years over budget and behind schedule.</p><p>Maria Bocanegra, president of Peoples Gas and North Shore Gas, told the committee that the increase is needed to complete a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/consumer-affairs/2025/02/20/peoples-gas-pipeline-program-icc-decision" target="_blank" >state mandate to replace aging pipes by 2025</a>, as well as offset higher operating costs from inflation.</p><p>She said the company is partnering with the city clerk’s office to help people find discounts for their bills, and is working to start a pilot for greener geothermal energy in the city.</p><p>In the hearing, Ald. Leni Manaa-Hoppenworth (48th) said the company has posted record profits while some of her constituents struggle to pay their gas bills.</p><p>Peoples Gas’ parent company, WEC Energy Group, reported $1.6 billion in profits in 2025, up from $1.5 billion the year prior. That’s after the company saw revenue grow eight times to nearly $10 billion between 2024 and 2025.</p><p>According to the Illinois Commerce Commission, <a class="Link" href="https://www.icc.illinois.gov/industry-reports/credit-collections-and-arrearages-reports/monthly-dashboard" target="_blank" ><u>more than 145,000 Chicago households</u></a> were at least 30 days behind on their gas bill in January — totaling nearly $10 million.</p><p>The gas company counters that the cost of the pipeline replacement project is necessary for safety.</p><p>The renewed pipe retirement project, set to kick off again in 10 Chicago neighborhoods this month, was expected to cost less than $2 billion to replace more than 2,000 miles of aging underground pipes by 2030. As of last year, it had already cost more than $3.3 billion, with more than 1,000 miles of particularly leak-prone pipe, or about half the project, left to cover — now by 2035.</p><div 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/73fe9b3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6883x3863+0+364/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F79%2F87%2Fb1f29e2f4292aeee224bd273d7dd%2Fpeoplesgashearing-260304-03.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/692f5ed/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6883x3863+0+364/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F79%2F87%2Fb1f29e2f4292aeee224bd273d7dd%2Fpeoplesgashearing-260304-03.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Abe Scarr, director of Illinois PIRG, speaks during a news conference outside City Hall in the Loop to advocate against a Peoples Gas rate hike, Tuesday, March 3, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Illinois Public Interest Research Group director Abe Scarr wasn’t confident the company could fulfill the mandate.</p><p>The company needs to replace 100 miles of pipe per year to stay on track, but historically completes 60 miles annually, according to a report from Scarr’s group.</p><p>Scarr and other advocates encouraged the gas company to lessen costs of the program by leaning into relining pipes, which can <a class="Link" href="https://nysearch.org/tech-brief-pdfs/TB13-12-2022-M2016-001-Cured-in-Place-Liner.pdf" target="_blank" >potentially double their lifespan</a>.</p><p>They also urged the company to invest in green energy alternatives as opposed to more gas infrastructure that they say will likely be phased out before it's paid off.</p><p>“This replacement first approach is too expensive,” Scarr said during the hearing. “This is more of a continuation than a break of People's Gas’ previous efforts.”</p>
entries.author: Violet Miller

entries.title: City Council committee backs tougher penalties for owners who neglect their pets.
entries.summary: <p>Calling animal cruelty and abuse a “precursor to crime” against people, the City Council's Committee on Public Safety also agreed to strengthen penalties against an array of animal neglect offenses.</p><p>Ald. Ray Lopez (15th), the Council’s resident dog lover and animal rights advocate, said the level of cruelty against dogs and other pets goes far beyond an occasional oversight.</p><p>Animals are being tied up outside and left in the cold with no fresh water, food, bedding or heat by owners who would “just as soon let them die and replace them with the next birth of a litter,” Lopez said.</p><p>“This ordinance addresses the starting point for so much of the public safety issues that we see because animals are the segue. If you can be that cruel to [a pet], it is not difficult to be cruel to another human being,” Lopez said.</p><p>Ald. Leni Manaa-Hoppenworth (48th) said she’s concerned about pet owners who may be “houseless” and need help to avoid being separated from their pets.</p><p>Lopez assured her there are “plenty of resources” to help pet owners who can’t afford housing or food for themselves to be “good owners, to be caring owners.”</p><p>““This is not going after the homeless population," Lopez added. "This specifically deals with those owners who are intentionally causing harm to their animals. … This is for individuals who clearly have no regard for the life in which they are in charge of and who intentionally will allow that life to suffer, regardless of what resources you provide them,” he said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>PAWS Chicago is a no-kill animal shelter focused on saving homeless pets.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Ald. Matt Martin (47th) asked if the ordinance has an “intent requirement” for an owner who is “negligent, but perhaps not intentionally” in failing to provide a dog, cat or other pet with medical care.</p><p>“Whether that individual is homeless or maybe they are of very modest means. They are, say, a person who has diminished faculties. Would this allow for that person to be fined $5,000 despite not knowingly withholding that care?” Martin said.</p><p>Lopez assured Martin the target is animal owners who “knowingly” neglect their pets and refuse to take advantage of the many resources available to them.</p><p>The city’s Commission on Animal Care and Control and organizations like PAWS routinely provides dog and cat food to owners struggling to put food on their own tables, Lopez said.</p><p>"There’s an immense amount of compassion among organizations and our own department for individuals in those types of circumstances. All of us would gladly help anybody who needs assistance. I’ve delivered with advocates food to help people. I’m not trying to hold them liable,” he said.</p><p>“But there are individuals who are not trying for that compassion, who are intentionally harming, neglecting, abusing or ignoring animals in their charge. … If you come to a house where you have a dog that is emaciated because the owner has tied them to the back pole and all they have is a square patch of dirt … that owner needs to be held accountable.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-800000" name="image-800000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/858f111/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F28%2F81%2F987d507c2a3d49dce6477ac60652%2Ftickets092817.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7dafb1e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F28%2F81%2F987d507c2a3d49dce6477ac60652%2Ftickets092817.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1852bad/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F28%2F81%2F987d507c2a3d49dce6477ac60652%2Ftickets092817.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Ald. Raymond Lopez (15th) championed an ordinance to set clear euthanasia policies for the city’s animal shelter." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1852bad/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F28%2F81%2F987d507c2a3d49dce6477ac60652%2Ftickets092817.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5802645/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F28%2F81%2F987d507c2a3d49dce6477ac60652%2Ftickets092817.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1852bad/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1024x575+0+54/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F28%2F81%2F987d507c2a3d49dce6477ac60652%2Ftickets092817.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago Ald. Raymond Lopez (15th) is a strong City Council advocate for animal protection. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Ald. Jesse Fuentes (26th) and Chris Taliafferro (29th) also raised concerns about getting too tough on dog and cat owners who are unhoused.</p><p>Fuentes also questioned whether a Commission on Animal Care and Control that has historically been underfunded and understaffed would have the employees to enforce the new ordinance.</p><p>“I have a neighbor who leaves two dogs outside year-round. I’ve reported it half a dozen times. Those pets live outside. Nothing’s been done,” Fuentes said.</p><p>Before a final vote by the full Council, Fuentes suggested adding language that would target only those neglectful and abusive pet owners with “social and financial means” and are “keeping a dog out in the backyard while they have a house.” Ideally, she would like an escalating scale of fines.</p><p>"A $5,000 fine I understand for beating, torturing, tormenting, knowingly poisoning" animals, Fuentes said.</p><p>"But for someone who may not have the financial means to resolve medical bills in seven days and they already don't have the financial means to resolve the health care of their pets seriously — how can they afford a $5,000 [fine]?" </p><p>Ald. Nick Sposato (38th) said it is “well-documented” that “cruel people start off being cruel to animals and they go on to humans.”</p><p>“We need to hit these people pretty hard" Sposato added. "Some of `em don’t get it. Hopefully, it’ll stop 'em. I don’t know if it will or won’t. But I have no sympathy for these people."</p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: Cubs' Cade Horton is just focused on preparing for the season — but could he start Opening Day?
entries.summary: <p>MESA, Ariz. — <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a> right-hander <b>Cade Horton</b>’s task this spring training is far different from previous years. Just last year he was focused on cleaning up his mechanics, with the hope of laying a foundation for a future call-up.</p><p>This year, spring training is simply a time to build up and prepare for the season. Horton is not only penciled into the rotation, but his elite second-half performance in 2025 put him in a strong position to compete for the Opening Day starter nod.</p><p>“It's obviously a really cool opportunity if it was to come,” Horton said after throwing three scoreless innings in an exhibition game against Team Italy on Tuesday. “But at the end of the day, when I go out there, my job is to help win games. And whether I throw the fifth day of the season or the first day, it's the same thing: going out there and trying to get outs.”</p><p>Horton’s coming off a glowing season that won him runner-up NL Rookie of the Year honors. He worked efficiently within late-season pitch limits to author a 2.67 ERA in 23 appearances. But a fractured rib sidelined him for the postseason.</p><p>In his sophomore season, a repeat of his 2025 performance but without guardrails would be an impressive next step.</p><p>“You can always adjust throughout the year on how much you want to push a guy,” pitching coach <b>Tommy Hottovy</b> said. “But definitely not going to baby him. That's the point, he's earned the right to go and go compete and be great.”</p><p>Horton came into spring training with his foot on the gas pedal. In his first live batting practice session, admittedly amped up, he hit 98 mph. In spring training starts, his fastball has been sitting right around a more sustainable 96 mph.</p><p>“When you're only throwing 20 [pitches] on a backfield, or a lower pitch count, guys will tend to push themselves a little bit more,” Hottovy said. “And I don't mind it, because we're human beings, right? Check that box. ‘Okay, didn't end the year healthy, I worked hard this offseason, let's see if everything I worked on is there.’ And then it is, and then you can kind of settle in. But he's done fantastic.”</p><h3>Carlson on the mend</h3><p>Outfielder <b>Dylan Carlson</b> did not need X-rays after getting drilled on the right elbow by a pitch Monday. He wasn’t in the Cubs’ lineup Tuesday against Team Italy, but he wasn’t scheduled to be, manager <b>Criag Counsell</b> said.</p><p>“I think we’re fine,” Counsell said.</p><p>When hitting, Carlson wears a guard on his front elbow, but he was hit by a 88.8 mph cutter on the inside of his back elbow in the fourth inning Monday. He exited in the bottom half of the inning, finishing his day with two hits in as many at-bats.</p><p>Carlson, a non-roster invitee, is <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/02/25/cubs-outfielder-michael-conforto-joins-evolving-bench-battle" >competing for a bench spot in camp</a>.</p><h3>Full circle</h3><p>When Counsell was a special assistant for the Brewers, between the end of his playing career and start of his managerial career, he chipped in on evaluating amateur players before the MLB Draft. Oregon State University outfielder <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/02/27/cubs-michael-conforto-motivated-by-2025-dissapointments" ><b>Michael Conforto</b></a>, now a 10-year veteran in camp with the Cubs, was the first player he was assigned to.</p><p>“He was projected to be a first-rounder, and was obviously a very talented kid,” Counsell said. “I learned that [amateur scouting is] an incredibly difficult job. But Michael was a great player. He stood out as a great player, for sure.”</p><p>Somewhere, Counsell said, he still has the positive report he wrote. Conforto was selected No. 10 overall by the Mets in the 2014 draft, and two picks later, the Brewers took left-handed pitcher <b>Kodi Medeiros</b>.</p><p></p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE"
