entries.author: Maddie Lee

entries.title: Northwestern professor and her family shelter in place in Qatar amidst Iranian airstrikes
entries.summary: <p>Christina Paschyn was at a dance class for her six-year-old daughter on Saturday in Doha, Qatar when she received a notification from the U.S. State Department that Israel had attacked Iran and she needed to shelter in place. </p><p>"We got the message, all of us grabbed our kids and basically made a dash to our Ubers or our cars to get home," Paschyn said. "And basically, from that point on, that's when the missile strikes started to occur."</p><p>On Saturday, after the U.S. and Israel launched a major attack on Iran the Qatari government began <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/video/iranian-missiles-intercepted-above-doha-693b6c5f39b6478e84ed1ab928f4c055" target="_blank" >intercepting Iranian missiles above Doha</a>. Following this, the U.S. Embassy in Doha issued a shelter-in-place order for personnel and recommended that all Americans do the same. </p><p>Since then Israel, the United Arab Emirates and Qatar have <a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/video/disruption-at-qatars-doha-airport-as-attack-on-iran-affects-flights-across-middle-east-and-beyond-f96b624323634b7bbe421440bf8031ca" target="_blank" >closed their airspace</a>, causing disruptions at airports across the Middle East as many attempt to leave the escalating conflict. On Monday, the State Department updated its travel advisory for Qatar ordering non-emergency U.S. government employees and their family members to leave Qatar due to the threat of armed conflict.</p><p>Paschyn has been hunkering down in her Doha home with her husband, six-year-old daughter and four-year-old son as the Qatari government continues to intercept Iranian missiles. </p><p>In the first days after the initial strikes, she said she told her children the rumbling they heard was just a bad thunderstorm, and that they had to stay inside. They believed it at first, she said. But after days of no rain they grew skeptical. On Monday, she said she told her kids countries are fighting with "fireworks."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-110000" name="image-110000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/57d9ef5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2100x1179+0+111/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc5%2F1a%2F55d85e7d411e8a6d887125029995%2Fqatar-6.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/eade724/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2100x1179+0+111/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc5%2F1a%2F55d85e7d411e8a6d887125029995%2Fqatar-6.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9af5054/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2100x1179+0+111/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc5%2F1a%2F55d85e7d411e8a6d887125029995%2Fqatar-6.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Christina Paschyn's children in their pantry" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9af5054/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2100x1179+0+111/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc5%2F1a%2F55d85e7d411e8a6d887125029995%2Fqatar-6.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e25c6de/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2100x1179+0+111/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc5%2F1a%2F55d85e7d411e8a6d887125029995%2Fqatar-6.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9af5054/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2100x1179+0+111/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fc5%2F1a%2F55d85e7d411e8a6d887125029995%2Fqatar-6.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Christina Paschyn’s children spend hours in their windowless pantry at their home in Doha, Qatar.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>For the past 15 years, Paschyn has been an associate professor at Northwestern University in Qatar teaching journalism. Since the strikes began on Saturday, the university has shifted to online classes, she said. </p><p>While the university has an official evacuation protocol to keep its community members safe, she said it's unclear at what point they would be evacuated. </p><p>In response to a question from the Sun-Times, Northwestern University issued a statement affirming the remote learning option and added, "Northwestern leadership and NU-Q leadership continue to communicate regularly with members of the Doha campus, whose safety is our priority."</p><p>In the meantime, she said she's continuing to teach her students virtually and living in "constant paranoia" as the sound of intercepted missiles explode throughout the night.</p><p>"What's interesting is that we do not have any air raid sirens that come on in the loudspeakers around the city," she said. "The most we get is these emergency texts from the Qatari government on our phones. We haven't received them, though in at least 24 hours."</p><p>Paschyn, a Ukrainian-American, filmed a documentary in Ukraine in 2024 examining the full-scale conflict through the eyes of Ukrainians attempting to survive the war.</p><p>"I learned all the tips and tricks to stay safe there, and we're applying that here now," she said.</p><p>During airstrikes, Paschyn said people have been told to go underground and stay away from windows. If unable to do that, they've been told to go to the lowest level of their homes and place curtains in front of windows to cushion the blow from possible debris. Her family's home has floor-to-ceiling windows and it doesn't have a basement. </p><p>"Whenever we get a phone alert, we hide in the pantry because it's the only really enclosed space without any windows," she said. </p><p>Most explosions and alerts happen overnight, she added. To preserve a sense of normalcy, she's keeping up the kids' nightly bedtime routine, putting them to sleep in their own beds. But then, in the middle of the night she and her husband move the children into their bedroom to keep everyone together. That way, if an alert comes through it's easier for them to all move to the pantry quickly, Paschyn said. </p><p>"You kind of just have to estimate whether it's worth running down the stairs or not at this point, but that's how we're trying to keep them safe," she said. </p><p>Paschyn and her husband continue to weigh all of their options. At this time, her family is planning to stay in Doha because of guidance from Northwestern. But as the conflict continues, she said that might change.</p><p>The family has filled up their car's gas tank, packed a small bag and applied for visas in Saudi Arabia, "just in case" they decide to make the eight-hour drive, she said.</p>
entries.author: Elleiana Green

entries.title: Former CPD officer gets 4 years in federal prison for bilking $14M from food stamps program
entries.summary: <p>A former Chicago police officer was sentenced Tuesday to four years in prison for defrauding a low-income food program.</p><p>Prosecutors say Hassan “Eric” Abdellatif, 37, illegally billed $14 million to the federal Women, Infants and Children program, or WIC, as owner of the El Milagro Mini Market and Harding Grocery in Chicago.</p><p>Abdellatif, who lost his CPD job after his 2021 arrest, asked for mercy before U.S. District Judge Jorge L. Alonso delivered the ruling at the Dirksen Federal Courthouse. In addition to prison time, the judge ordered Abdellatif pay $8 million in restitution.</p><p>Defense attorneys said they were pleased Abdellatif got less than the 8 to 10 years federal prosecutors asked for. They said they would appeal the ruling.</p><p>“While we’re pleased the judge ruled substantially below the guidelines, my client will spend 4 years in prison and come out $8 million in debt,” Steven Hunter, one of Abdellatif’s attorneys in the case, told the Sun-Times after the hearing. “We’re not pleased about that.”</p><p>In 2021, Abdellatif was among nine owners and workers at several Chicago-area convenience stores charged with accepting checks from the WIC program for ineligible items at the stores, often at inflated prices.</p><p>After a jury trial last April, Abdellatif was convicted of wire fraud, fraudulently obtaining government benefits and failure to file government taxes.</p><p>Federal prosecutors said they calculated the extent of Abdellatif's fraud by comparing his stores' WIC billings to that of similar stores. The fraud happened from 2010 to 2018, prosecutors said. Abdellatif became an officer in 2018, two months after the fraud ended, according to prosecutors.</p><p>Abdellatif appeared for his sentencing in a gray suit as he frequently spoke with his attorneys, rubbed his hands and looked toward family members in attendance supporting him. At one point, he was seen looking to the ceiling and mouthing what appeared to be a prayer.</p><p>Abdellatif told the judge he already faced several consequences from his arrest and conviction, such as the loss of his police career and the emotional hardship brought onto his family.</p><p>“For all the pain and disappointment this caused … I am truly sorry,” Abdellatif said. “I regret the circumstances that brought me here.”</p><p>Prior to his ruling, the judge noted that Abdellatif is educated, hard working and supportive of his family. </p><p>“That bodes well for [his] rehabilitation," the judge said.</p>
entries.author: Mohammad Samra

entries.title: What we know about Rev. Jesse Jackson's death and plans to honor his life
entries.summary: <p>The Rev. Jesse Jackson <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/obituaries/2026/02/17/rev-jesse-jackson-civil-rights-leader-rainbow-push-obituary" target="_blank" >“died peacefully”</a> Feb. 17, his family said during a news conference. He was 84. </p><p>Jackson is a Chicago icon known for his work as a civil rights leader. "All are welcome" for services scheduled this week, according to his family.</p><h3>When are services?</h3><p><b></b><b></b></p><p></p><p><b></b></p><p> </p><p><b></b><a class="Link" href="https://www.jessejacksonlegacy.com" target="_blank" ><b></b></a><b></b></p><p>Omega Psi Phi Fraternity Inc., a Divine Nine fraternity that Jackson belonged to, will hold a celebration of life service at 5 p.m. <b>Thursday</b> at Rainbow PUSH's Kenwood headquarters. Grand officers, district officers and various members of the fraternity will be in attendance, said a district representative. </p><p>A "People's Celebration" in Chicago will be held at 10 a.m. <b>Friday</b>, at House of Hope, 752 E. 114th St. Doors open at 9 a.m. Additional parking will be available at nearby businesses. Shuttles will transport people to the venue.</p><p>Janet Pickett, general manager at House of Hope, said the church is planning for more than 9,000 people to attend. The venue seats 9,600. There is no word yet on who will lead the services, she said. Ushers and personnel for the event will be staffed by House of Hope and Rainbow PUSH. </p><p>Private homegoing services are scheduled for 10 a.m. <b>Saturday</b> at Rainbow PUSH.</p><p>Dr. Charles Jenkins, pastor of Rev. Jackson's home church, Fellowship Baptist Church will co-officiate both services with the Rev. James T. Meeks, the emeritus pastor of Salem Baptist Missionary Church. A news release said that the two "are close, trusted friends of the Jackson family and will lead services featuring family, local, national and international leaders, along with entertainers scheduled to perform musical selections."</p><p>Participants at the celebrations will include singer and actress Jennifer Hudson and gospel singers Bebe Winans and Pastor Marvin Winans, according to the news release. Chicago Mayor Brandon Johnson and Illinois Gov. JB Pritzker also will give speeches.</p><p>Former Democratic presidents <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/03/03/three-former-presidents-clinton-obama-biden-attending-chicago-service-honoring-rev-jesse-jackson" >Joe Biden, Barack Obama and Bill Clinton are expected to attend</a> the Friday service. The Jackson family also invited President Donald Trump and former president George W. Bush, but it's unclear whether they are planning to attend. </p><p>Relatives of Jackson said that "all are welcome," regardless of political party.</p><p>"Do not bring your politics, out of respect to Rev. Jesse Jackson and the life that he lived, to these homegoing services. They come respectful and come to say thank you," Jesse Jackson Jr. said during a media event in front of the Jackson family home. "But these homegoing services are welcome to all — Democrat, Republican, liberal and conservative, right wing, left wing, because his life is broad enough to cover the full spectrum of what it means to be an American."</p><p>The family will livestream all services at JesseJacksonLegacy.com. </p><h3>What services have already taken place to honor Rev. Jackson?</h3><p>In Chicago, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2026/02/26/rev-jesse-jackson-lying-in-repose-rainbow-push-chicago" >Jackson lay in repose</a> on Feb. 26 and 27 at Rainbow PUSH.</p><p>On Monday, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/02/the-rev-jesse-jackson-greenville-south-carolina-colombia-greenville8-lying-in-state-chicago" >a full day of recognition took place</a> at and near the South Carolina state capitol. He lay in repose at the statehouse in Columbia before private, public and church services took place throughout the day. A formal service at 10 a.m. was followed by a tribute service at the Brookland Baptist Church.</p><p>Sprays of flowers announced him as "Son of South Carolina," as lines of people waited to pay their respects. </p><p>Greenville's state representative, Chandra Dillard, thanked his children for "bringing their dad back to South Carolina."</p><h3>How have Chicagoans paid their respects?</h3><p>Thousands gathered at <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2026/02/26/rev-jesse-jackson-lying-in-repose-rainbow-push-chicago" >Rainbow PUSH headquarters last week</a> to pay their respects to Jackson. The family procession arrived after 8 a.m. As the morning wore on, hundreds were lined up to show their gratitude for Jackson and be a part of a historical moment. </p><p>Mayor Brandon Johnson and the Rev. Al Sharpton were in attendance Thursday to pay their respects.</p><p>"We spent a lifetime together," Sharpton said outside Rainbow PUSH headquarters. "He raised a generation of us, and he never gave up, even when he was sick." </p><p>Crowds included <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago/2026/02/27/jesse-jacksons-death-inspires-young-people-more-present-community" >several Black youth who were inspired by Jackson</a> to be more present in their community. </p><p>Among them was Nadiyah Spikener, 24, who said Jackson's death could be a catalyst for young people to learn more about their history. </p><p>"This may be a moment to realize that maybe we need to do more for ourselves, and do more for our community like he has, and just start somewhere," she said. </p><h3>What hotels are recommended for those traveling to Chicago for the events?</h3><p>Those traveling to Chicago to attend services are encouraged to stay at the Hyatt Regency McCormick Place Chicago at 2233 S. Martin Luther King Drive, according to the family's website. Rooms at the hotel have been reserved within a hotel block, with limited availability. </p><h3>Where can people park?</h3><p>For those hoping to attend the Jackson lying in repose events, street parking is available at Rainbow PUSH on South Drexel Boulevard, East 49th Street, East Hyde Park Boulevard and East 49th Street. </p><p>For the People's Celebration, parking will be available at House of Hope's parking lot on 113th Street. </p><h3>What security measures will be in place?</h3><p>At each event, security checks and magnetometers will be in place. To smooth the process, the Jackson family's website asks that individuals limit items they bring with them. Weapons, signs and any items that could disrupt services are prohibited.</p><p>For more information on the scheduled services visit <a class="Link" href="https://www.jessejacksonlegacy.com/" target="_blank" >jessejacksonlegacy.com</a>. </p><h3>Where will the Rev. Jesse Jackson be buried?</h3><p>Jackson will be buried at <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/curious-city/2025/06/04/prejudice-follows-us-to-the-grave-the-segregated-past-of-chicago-cemeteries#:~:text=Mayor%20Harold%20Washington%20is%20buried,bring%20it%20to%20an%20end." target="_blank" >Oak Woods Cemetery</a>, the largest cemetery on the South Side, in Greater Grand Crossing. Former Chicago Mayor Harold Washington, journalist and civil rights activist Ida B. Wells and Olympic gold medalist Jesse Owens are among the notables buried there.</p><h3>How did he die?</h3><p>While his family has not given an official cause of death nor said where he died, Jackson had been declining in health for a decade before his death. In 2015,<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2017/11/17/18375473/rev-jesse-jackson-announces-parkinson-s-diagnosis" > Jackson was initially diagnosed with Parkinson’s disease</a>, but he revealed last April that he actually had progressive supranuclear palsy.</p><h3>How is the political world reacting to his death?</h3><p>Political figures from across the country have weighed in to pay their respects and honor his legacy. </p><p>In a joint statement, former President Barack Obama and former first lady Michelle Obama wrote: "Michelle and I will always be grateful for Jesse's lifetime of service, and the friendship our families share. We stood on his shoulders. We send our deepest condolences to the Jackson family and everyone in Chicago and beyond who knew and loved him." </p><p>President Donald Trump also extended his sympathies to Jackson's family in a statement posted to Truth Social last week. </p><p>"I knew him well, long before becoming President," he said. "He was very gregarious — Someone who truly loved people! Despite the fact that I am falsely and consistently called a Racist by the Scoundrels and Lunatics on the Radical Left, Democrats ALL, it was always my pleasure to help Jesse along the way."</p><p>Chicago leaders, including Mayor Brandon Johnson and Gov. JB Pritzker, also shared their condolences. </p><p>"In honor of Rev. Jackson's service, and as our city mourns, the Chicago flag will lower to half-staff," Johnson wrote in a statement. </p><p>Pritzker also ordered all flags across the state to fly at half-staff to honor him in a statement posted on social media.</p><p>"He broke down barriers, inspired generations, and kept hope alive. Our state, nation, and world are better due to his years of service," Pritzker said.</p><h3>How are Chicagoans reacting to his death?</h3><p>Across the city, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/02/17/jesse-jackson-chicago-remembers" >Chicago residents mourned Jackson’s death</a> and urged that his legacy continue. </p><p>Martin Travis, a congregant of Apostolic Church of God in Woodlawn, said in his youth, his parents brought him to demonstrations organized by Operation Breadbasket, a movement Jackson led. </p><p>Now, 64, Travis remembers Jackson as "instrumental in fighting for justice and freedom for all people."</p><p>Chloe Gould, 41, a Bronzeville resident, said that his death hit "like you lost a family member." </p><p>In the wake of his death, she said she worries about the future of civil rights activism, as other activists and leaders have not stepped in to fill his shoes. </p><p>"Who is gonna be the civil rights activist now?" she asked. </p>
entries.author: Nicole Jeanine Johnson

entries.title: City Council committee rejects ordinance to hold parents responsible for childrens' lawbreaking
entries.summary: <p>Chicago parents will not be held accountable for curfew violations, street racing, drug, alcohol and firearms offenses committed by their children.</p><p>The City Council’s Committee on Public Safety made that decision Tuesday, snuffing out an ordinance championed by 15th Ward Ald. Ray Lopez for the purpose of “creating better parents,” as he put it.</p><p>Lopez tried to keep the ordinance alive by offering to hold it, allowing time to entertain amendments that would have softened the potential legal blow to parents and legal guardians.</p><p>But opposition was so strong, a "do not pass" motion by 40th Ward Ald. Andre Vasquez prevailed, 10-5.</p><p>The debate showcased the Council's political divide. Conservative alderpersons, who are among the police union’s staunchest supporters, want to get tougher on crime. Democratic Socialists and other progressives seem more, or at least equally, concerned about preserving individual rights.</p><p>Lopez argued the Council already has agreed to hold parents responsible for spray-painting and graffiti by their kids and that doing so has led to a “precipitous decline” in that vandalism.</p><p>The results would be the same, Lopez added, if parents were held accountable for “willfully or knowingly” allow a minor under their care to violate a broader array of crimes that include curfew violations; illegal drag racing, drifting or jumping on vehicles; illicit drug or alcohol use and access to firearms or paintball guns.</p><p>Parents accused of “contributing to the delinquency of a minor” who commits any one of those crimes would have faced fines of at least $1,000, community service and family counseling. In a last-ditch effort to salvage the ordinance, Lopez offered to change “all of the above” to a choice between those three penalties.</p><p>“I do not want to criminalize parenting. What I want to do is wake parents up. Too often, we have youth running around with no guidance, no parental awareness,” Lopez said.</p><p>“They should be fully aware where their children are. If they are not, or they are knowingly dismissing what their children are up to, they should be held accountable, plain and simple. … The goal isn’t about the money. The goal is about better parenting.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-840000" name="image-840000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4311492/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7b%2Ffa%2F6e040d2d4138941c6c2feb5e492d%2Floopshooting-112225-27.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ac5ba01/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7b%2Ffa%2F6e040d2d4138941c6c2feb5e492d%2Floopshooting-112225-27.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/313c168/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7b%2Ffa%2F6e040d2d4138941c6c2feb5e492d%2Floopshooting-112225-27.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Chicago police investigate the scene where multiple people were shot outside the Chicago Theatre in the Loop in November 2025. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/313c168/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7b%2Ffa%2F6e040d2d4138941c6c2feb5e492d%2Floopshooting-112225-27.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2915f0a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7b%2Ffa%2F6e040d2d4138941c6c2feb5e492d%2Floopshooting-112225-27.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/313c168/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7b%2Ffa%2F6e040d2d4138941c6c2feb5e492d%2Floopshooting-112225-27.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago police investigate the scene where multiple people were shot outside the Chicago Theatre in the Loop Nov. 21. The shooting occurred during a “teen takeover” downtown. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Police Committee Chair Chris Taliaferro (29th), a former police sergeant, was the first to object, saying he "can't support" an ordinance that is "looking to hold a parent liable as if they committed the act themselves, which I believe would fall short of being constitutional.”</p><p>Assistant Corporation Counsel Kristopher Kolb said he was not aware of any other section of the city code "that would authorize imprisonment for vicarious liablity.”</p><p>Northwest Side Ald. Nick Sposato (38th) acknowledged the legal question was a “tough one,” adding: “You don’t want to hit up parents too hard.”</p><p>He nevertheless agreed with Lopez that it’s high-time to get tough on parents.</p><p>Sposato kept pushing, even after Kolb said someone who isn't committing a crime themselves "is generally not liable for a crime committed by another person, even if they are related.”</p><p>“I know what’s going on," Sposato said. "There’s bad parents. Some parents have their challenges. I don’t doubt that. They have kids that are out of control. They’re at wit’s end. They don’t know what to do. But, some parents enable some of these kids to do what they do."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a50000" name="image-a50000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9c7ebbd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6715x3769+0+354/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9e%2F36%2F0a09d93998d38733ad9dd9790ece%2Fhoyne-12xx23-3.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2eb0100/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6715x3769+0+354/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9e%2F36%2F0a09d93998d38733ad9dd9790ece%2Fhoyne-12xx23-3.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9c36961/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6715x3769+0+354/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9e%2F36%2F0a09d93998d38733ad9dd9790ece%2Fhoyne-12xx23-3.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A woman points to the skid marks in a McKinley Park intersection that's been the site of street takeovers. where street takeovers happen, with skid marks on the pavement that are a sign of drivers doing burnouts. The resident, who spoke to the Sun-Times in December 2023, said drivers keep her and her husband up until the wee hours of the morning. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9c36961/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6715x3769+0+354/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9e%2F36%2F0a09d93998d38733ad9dd9790ece%2Fhoyne-12xx23-3.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/954ac3e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6715x3769+0+354/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9e%2F36%2F0a09d93998d38733ad9dd9790ece%2Fhoyne-12xx23-3.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9c36961/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6715x3769+0+354/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9e%2F36%2F0a09d93998d38733ad9dd9790ece%2Fhoyne-12xx23-3.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A woman points to the skid marks from burned rubber in a McKinley Park intersection that’s been the site of street takeovers.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The political and philosophical divide in the City Council also stalled approval of another crime-fighting ordinance championed by Lopez — this one dramatically cracking down on anyone who commits any of a broad swath of crimes while concealing or attempting to conceal their identify by wearing a mask, hoodie or similar clothing.</p><p>Ever since the COVID-19 pandemic, Lopez said criminals have taken to wearing masks, scarves, hoodies and other clothing to make it difficult for police and witnesses to identify them.</p><p>“We see time and again that people are being carjacked by someone wearing masks. Stores being broken into. … Ten thousand robberies against small businesses. We have ATM rings where we see video … (of thieves) wearing masks. We have to address this,” Lopez said.</p><p>Vasquez joined Alds. Matt Martin (47th) and Leni Manaa-Hoppenworth (48th) in arguing that Lopez was once again going too far.</p><p>Vasquez, who wore a hoodie to the hearing, said: "I've got problems with the hoodie thing in general — and not just because I'm wearing one. ... The level of profiling and the level of racial profiling that's occurred with people wearing hoodies and other clothing is a concern."</p><p>Sposato was exasperated by the debate.</p><p>"Boy, did this get twisted around," he said. "How 'bout somebody who's wearing a hoodie when it's 80 degrees out? ... These restaurant owners are losing everything. These scumbags are going in there robbing 'em blind, concealing their identity." </p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: Cardinals will release two-time Pro Bowl QB Kyler Murray
entries.summary: <p>TEMPE, Ariz. — The Arizona Cardinals have informed two-time Pro Bowl quarterback Kyler Murray that they plan to release him at the beginning of the new league year on March 11, a person familiar with the decision told The Associated Press.</p><p>The person spoke to the AP on condition of anonymity because the move can't be officially announced until next week.</p><p>Murray, the No. 1 overall pick in the 2019 draft — who is owed $36.8 million in guaranteed money in 2026 — will be free to sign with any team once he's released.</p><p>The 28-year-old posted a goodbye message to Arizona's fans on social media, expressing regret that he couldn't have more success with the franchise. He led the team to the playoffs just once during his seven-year tenure — a wild-card loss in 2021.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">To everyone that supported me and showed kindness to my family and I during my time in AZ, from the bottom of my heart, thank you. <br><br>I wanted nothing more than to be the one to end the 77 year drought for this organization, I am sorry I failed us. I wish this community and my…</p>— Kyler Murray (@K1) <a href="https://twitter.com/K1/status/2028937232057549312?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 3, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>"To everyone that supported me and showed kindness to my family and I during my time in AZ, from the bottom of my heart, thank you," Murray posted. "I wanted nothing more than to be the one to end the 77-year drought for this organization, I am sorry I failed us. I wish this community and my brothers nothing but the best."</p><p>He continued: "I am no stranger to adversity, I am prepared for whatever's next. I trust in God and my work ethic. I truly believe my best ball is in front of me and I look forward to proving it. Godspeed."</p><p>Murray played in just five games last season — throwing for 962 yards, six touchdowns and three interceptions — before suffering a foot injury against the Tennessee Titans. The Cardinals initially thought Murray would only miss a few weeks, but backup Jacoby Brissett played well in his absence, creating a quarterback controversy.</p><p>Murray was eventually placed on injured reserve and missed the rest of the season. The Cardinals finished with a 3-14 record and fired coach Jonathan Gannon.</p><p>Murray arrived in Arizona with huge expectations after winning the Heisman Trophy at Oklahoma in 2019, and at times he delivered.</p><p>He was the AP Offensive Rookie of the Year, providing multiple electrifying highlights such as the so-called "Hail Murray" in 2020, when he connected with DeAndre Hopkins for a game-winning touchdown against the Buffalo Bills on the final play of the game.</p><p>The pinnacle of his Cardinals' tenure came in 2021, when the team jumped out to a 10-2 record by early December. But Arizona faded down the stretch, losing four of its final five games before getting bounced by the Los Angeles Rams 34-11 in the wild-card round.</p><p>The Cardinals signed Murray to $230.5 million, five-year deal in 2022 with $160 million guaranteed, but the honeymoon after that commitment was short lived. A few weeks after signing the deal, the franchise removed a strange clause in his contract, which mandated four hours of "independent study" during game weeks.</p><p>The kerfuffle called into question Murray's work ethic, and his relationship with the franchise was never quite the same.</p><p>Murray tore his ACL against the New England Patriots in 2022, which caused him to miss the rest of that season and a big chunk of the 2023 schedule. Coach Kliff Kingsbury was fired after the 2022 season and Gannon was hired to replace him.</p><p>The Gannon-Murray relationship showed some promise in 2024 when the Cardinals improved to 8-9 and were in the playoff hunt until the final few weeks of the season.</p><p>But 2025 fell apart quickly, hastening the end of Murray's time in the desert.<br></p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Fight against child abuse made more difficult by Epstein scandal
entries.summary: <p>The children arrive every day.</p><p>"Monday we had 11 kids come in. Tuesday we had 10," said Char Rivette, chief executive officer of the Chicago Children's Advocacy Center. "Wednesday only six. Every day, brand new cases. I see them and think: How would this child feel if they thought they're being trivialized? Especially teenagers. How painful that is, to feel those feelings, being hurt so badly by multiple people throughout your life."</p><p>Hurt by being sexually abused. The few cases that enter the system — most are never reported — begin at the advocacy center at 1240 S. Damen, a colorful facility designed to be comforting to young victims.</p><p>"We investigate all allegations of child sexual abuse in the City of Chicago, with the Chicago Police Department and DCFS," said Rivette. "We come together to make sure these kids are heard, and move forward with investigations. We interview kids, make sure they have everything they need. We also do education, outreach and prevention. We want to put ourselves out of business."</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-c30000" name="module-c30000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Opinion bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Opinion</h2></div> </div><p>We were talking because, with the Epstein files straddling the news, a salacious national scandal of the rich and powerful, and pedophilia now a casually flung political slur, I began to wonder how this affects people working in the trenches every day trying to help anonymous children who have been molested. I reached out to Rivette and asked how this affects her ability to do her job.</p><p>"Two things," she said. First: "I sigh, because this is a problem that has been historically huge in the world, especially in the United States. Then it gets sensationalized. We've been hearing about Epstein for years and years, and it can be really distracting from what happens. The sensationalism it creates in the media makes victims even more reluctant to come forward, because it doesn't feel safe. They're afraid they're not going to be believed, and nothing is going to change. ... This Epstein thing makes it seem more scary."</p><p>Second, she realizes: "OK, here's an opportunity to bring this to the forefront. I try to see it as an opportunity to educate."</p><p>So let's educate. What do people need to know? </p><p>Child sex abuse is common.</p><p>"One in four women report being sexually abused as kids," she said. "It's just so underreported."</p><p>The assailants are not jet-set real estate millionaires who sweet talk victims off playgrounds.</p><p>"The stats are that 90, 95 percent of children know their abuser," said Rivette. "A lot of those folks are relatives or people close to the family. Mom's boyfriend. A neighbor. Older cousins. That's far more problematic than being trafficked by a stranger or being picked up on the street. ... We see up to 2,000 cases a year here. There are kids coming in because of dad, cousin, uncle, boyfriend. That is the primary perpetrator. That is where we need to focus our energies."</p><p>So how can parents protect their kids?</p><p>"One is strong, healthy parenting," Rivette said. "We do lots of training here. For parents, we teach them to be very open and honest with their kids. Believe them when they express being uncomfortable around others. Keep clear and open lines of communication.</p><p>Another level, she said is "to teach children about their own body autonomy, and consent. Notice grooming behavior. Notice when they are being treated disrespectfully. Tell their parents when that is happening."</p><p>So much focus is put on teaching girls not to be victims, so little on teaching boys not to be predators.</p><p>"I'm a 60-year-old woman," Rivette said. "I grew up in the '60s and '70. We were always taught, what do you do when you leave the house. How do you protect yourself. Don't get drunk at a bar. All this stuff. My brother was never taught. It's not a strong message we hear. We are missing that part. Not only to protect themselves from being exploited, to teach all children of all genders to respect each other. Being a caring person isn't emasculating."</p><p>What should pop out of the Epstein saga is not the famous names, but that so many rich and powerful individuals knew and did nothing. </p><p>"The thing that drives me crazy about the Epstein situation and others is decades and decades of overlooking that behavior," Rivette said. "All those people witness those things, and the witnesses simply didn't do anything. We as adults, as good citizens and people who are responsible, it's our job to notice those signs.</p><p>"Be educated on signs of grooming — the coach pulling a kid aside to isolate them, give special gifts, rides home. Say something. You don't need to call the police. Say, 'Hey dude. I see that.' Tell that child, ''You know, we have rules here.' Observe. Report. Do not turn a blind eye. Believe these kids."</p>
entries.author: Neil Steinberg

entries.title: Friend of the White Sox Jake Peavy gets positive vibes from the team
entries.summary: <p>GLENDALE, Ariz. — Jake Peavy is bullish on the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" >White Sox</a> this season. The former Sox right-hander was in camp on Monday at Camelback Ranch in his role as an MLB Network commentator. It’s 30 clubs, 30 camps and the White Sox are high in the rotation as the network crew attacks all 15 Cactus League camps in 15 days.</p><p>Peavy said he likes the roster changes on the club and enjoys the spring vibe.</p><p>He has friends and former teammates here: manager Will Venable, general manager Chris Getz, and assistant GM Josh Barfield. Peavy played with Venable and Barfield in San Diego before the Padres traded him to the White Sox, where he played with Getz. Geoff Head was the strength and conditioning coach when Peavy finished his career in San Francisco. Head is now in the same role with the Sox.</p><p>“I’ve got good sources inside this organization and people I believe in,” Peavy told the Sun-Times. “When you see what they did in the second half, and you add [Munetaka] Murakami and Austin Hayes, if <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/02/will-venable-says-andrew-benintendi-will-be-back-in-action-this-week" >[Andrew] Benintendi</a> bounces back — I don’t know if they can pull off winning the American League Central and beat out Detroit or Cleveland this year, but I can see the makings of a Central champion in the very near future.”</p><p>These are heady words. The White Sox have lost in excess of 100 games the past three seasons, including 102 under Venable last year and a record 121 in 2024.</p><p>Peavy pitched for the White Sox from 2009 to 2013. He was part of a 2013 trade deadline deal to Boston and won that year’s World Series with the Red Sox over the St. Louis Cardinals. The next midseason he was shipped to the Giants and won the 2014 World Series with them over the Kansas City Royals.</p><p>Early in his White Sox tenure in 2010, Peavy had one of the most famous experimental surgeries ever on his right shoulder. He had a detached latissimus dorsi muscle and Chicago orthopedic surgeon Anthony Romo reattached the muscle to the bone via tiny hooks and with a thread akin to the strength of fishing line. He was able to pitch for six more seasons and went 36-29 with a 4.00 ERA in 83 starts for the White Sox.</p><p>He was a bulldog, Venable recalled. “When he made a mistake on the mound you could hear him cursing and grunting in the outfield.”</p><p>After retiring , Peavy has played guitar and sung in his own band, plus become a personality in his own right on network television. His positive thoughts on the White Sox may come as much from his loyalty to the team and his old friends as from any objectivity.</p><p>“I love what they have on both sides of the ball for the foreseeable future,” he said. “You can write that any way you want to, but it’s the truth.”</p><p>As far as Venable is concerned, he joined the Padres to play the outfield late in the 2008 season and played on and off with Peavy until the pitcher waived his no-trade clause and was dealt to the White Sox at the 2009 trade deadline.</p><p>Venable, in his second season managing the White Sox, was an associate manager under Bruce Bochy with the Texas Rangers and won the 2023 World Series with them over the Arizona Diamondbacks. He was then hired to lead the White Sox back from the wilderness. </p><p>“Will just has incredible experience,” Peavy said. “I played with Will. Will was a smart baseball player. I could see him managing when we played together. But then you take all that and you go through the coaching ranks the way he has, that’s what it’s all about. You take what you learned from Boch and other coaches and blend it into your own style.</p><p>“He’s an unbelievable communicator. I just think the world of Will.”</p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" entries.author: Barry M. Bloom

entries.title: Profesores y estudiantes de DePaul piden a la universidad revertir la decisión de cerrar su museo de arte
entries.summary: <p>Varios profesores de DePaul han escrito una <a class="Link" href="https://openletter.earth/letter-opposing-the-closing-of-dpam-f02e5d5a?limit=0" target="_blank" >carta abierta</a> pidiendo a la universidad que reconsidere su decisión de cerrar su museo de arte. La universidad confirmó la semana pasada que planea cerrar su museo de forma permanente el 30 de junio.</p><p>El sábado, seis miembros de la facultad organizaron la carta publicada en línea. Al momento de la impresión, más de 1,500 estudiantes, profesores, personal y exalumnos habían agregado sus nombres a la carta.</p><p>El museo es considerado un importante espacio local para artistas subrepresentados. Su colección de más de 4,000 objetos incluye fotografías de artistas como Andy Warhol y los residentes de Chicago Dawoud Bey y Paul D’Amato. El museo también tiene un considerable acervo de objetos de África Occidental y arte latino.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-730000" name="module-730000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>La carta abierta está firmada por líderes del arte de Chicago, incluidos el gerente de colecciones del Museum of Contemporary Art, el curador del Barry Art Museum de la Universidad Old Dominion y bibliotecarios de la escuela del Art Institute.</p><p>La declaración pide a la universidad que reconsidere el cierre del museo de arte por dos motivos: “la base del valor pedagógico interno y sustantivo que tiene la colección del museo” y “la contribución externa que hace el museo para fortalecer el perfil de DePaul como institución tan comprometida con las artes que construyó un ‘corredor de artes’, que vincula al museo con la recientemente construida Escuela de Música, el edificio de Artes y Letras, y la Escuela de Teatro”.</p><p>Sean Kirkland, profesor de filosofía en DePaul y principal organizador de la campaña de redacción de cartas, dijo que estaba “impactado” y que “no esperaba” el cierre del museo.</p><p>La noticia llega dos meses después de que en diciembre la Universidad DePaul<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/12/16/la-universidad-depaul-despide-a-114-empleados" target="_blank" >despidiera a 114 empleados</a>.</p><p>“Habíamos escuchado, por supuesto, que había presiones financieras y ese tipo de cosas, pero realmente no esperábamos que el cierre fuera la respuesta a esas presiones”, agregó Kirkland.</p><p>El objetivo de la carta es “intentar generar algún tipo de clamor público sobre el cierre y ver si podemos revertir la decisión de la administración”, explicó.</p><p>Si bien la universidad aún no ha respondido a la petición, el grupo planea presentarla formalmente a tres altos administradores esta semana: el presidente Rob Manuel, la rectora Salma Ghanem y la vicepresidenta ejecutiva Sherri Sidler.</p><p>Kirkland dijo que el museo es una pérdida para los estudiantes de su clase de filosofía ambiental, quienes normalmente lo visitan cuando hay exposiciones relacionadas con el mundo natural.</p><p>“Muchos de nosotros buscamos cada oportunidad para presentar a nuestros estudiantes el espacio del museo y la colección”, afirmó Kirkland. “Queremos confrontar a nuestros estudiantes con cosas que los empujen fuera de la esfera de utilidad y les hagan pensar de manera diferente y experimentar el mundo de manera diferente. Y eso me parece precisamente lo que hace el arte”.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Ambar Colón

entries.title: Blackhawks center Jason Dickinson is prepared for possible trade: 'My game is well-built for playoffs'
entries.summary: <p>WINNIPEG, Manitoba — Jason Dickinson felt the absence of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/02/blackhawks-trade-connor-murphy-oilers-stan-bowman-nhl-deadline" target="_blank" >just-traded</a> Connor Murphy around the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> on Tuesday morning.</p><p>"It feels weird," Dickinson said. "The presence of him was hard to quantify because he was just so steady and so reliable in so many ways — not just on the ice but as a leader in the room. You could always look to Murph."</p><p>Dickinson also felt the spotlight of Hawks trade speculation shifting from Murphy to himself.</p><p>Three days away from the NHL trade deadline Friday, general manager Kyle Davidson is likely exploring the market for Dickinson and Ilya Mikheyev, two forwards whose defensive acumen could appeal to contenders.</p><p>Davidson knows he needs to free up roster spots for prospects graduating into the league, and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/01/27/blackhawks-jason-dickinson-nhl-trade-deadline-oliver-moore-joy" target="_blank" >Dickinson knows</a> Davidson knows that.</p><p>"It's been on the radar for the better part of a month now," Dickinson said. "Everybody is obviously wondering about the situation, given that I have an expiring contract. It's a natural progression in the league. </p><p>"Whether something is going to happen or not, I truly don't know. But I know Kyle will be clear and upfront with me when something does come to him or if they're pursuing something. He's been nothing but world-class with me. The next three days could be interesting."</p><p>Dickinson has been traded twice before — from the Stars to Canucks in July 2021 and from the Canucks to Hawks in October 2022 — but never at the deadline. </p><p>So this is a new experience for him, trying to compartmentalize uncertainty about the future while playing games on a regular basis. If he does get dealt, however, Tuesday's matchup against the Jets will mark his final appearance as a Hawk.</p><p>"[Dickinson is] a great human, really even-keeled, got a great head on his shoulders, very self-accountable, looks in the mirror every day and wants to better himself," Hawks coach Jeff Blashill said. "He's been a really good piece for us. We'll see what happens. I have no control over that, and neither does he.</p><p>"I wish we were 10 points [into a playoff spot]. If we were 10 points in, we wouldn't have to talk about this stuff. But that's the goal next year."</p><p>Dickinson wishes the same. It's hard for him to believe he hasn't been in the playoffs since 2019 and 2020, his first two full NHL seasons. A silver lining of getting traded — for him, just like it was for Murphy — would be possibly ending that drought.</p><p>"I had older guys [in Dallas] tell me, 'Savor it because you may never get this chance again,'" Dickinson said. "I didn't believe them at the time. Here I am, six years later, and I haven't seen the playoffs since. The stories guys tell you, they really do ring true. </p><p>"You have to savor those moments because the playoffs are [expletive] hard to get into, and winning a round is really hard to do. When you get the opportunity, appreciate it and realize what you just accomplish. So if I got the chance, I would be more than thrilled."</p><p>The Kings are one team to watch, although they're far from assured to make the playoffs. They've closely scouted the Hawks lately, and Davidson reportedly attended the Kings-Avalanche game Monday. </p><p>They entered Tuesday four points below the playoff line, but it would make sense for them to try to make a push in Anze Kopitar's final season, especially after acquiring Artemi Panarin. Injuries to Kevin Fiala and Andrei Kuzmenko have depleted their forward depth; Dickinson or Mikheyev could supplement it.</p><p>The Wild and Hurricanes are two other teams potentially seeking help at center. </p><p>"I think my game is well-built for playoffs," Dickinson added. "It's simple; it's direct. Teams can want something like that to bolster depth and give them strength on the [penalty kill] instead of big-fish hunting for a dynamic player."</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore entries.title: Los votantes de Chicago y los suburbios se preparan para las elecciones primarias del 17 de marzo
entries.summary: <p>Anthony Hamilton, residente de Andersonville, se detuvo el lunes en un centro de votación en el Loop para entregar su boleta de voto por correo. Dijo que le gusta llenar su boleta con anticipación para asegurarse de que su voto cuente.</p><p>“Vivo en el Distrito 9, y hay 10 personas postulándose. Así que en este momento, se siente realmente importante tener a alguien que hable sobre tantos problemas que tú, como, te preocupas”.</p><p>Hamilton, de 33 años, fue uno de los muchos votantes en el área de Chicago que acudieron a votar, ya que los <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/02/mas-sitios-de-votacion-temprana-abren-el-lunes-en-chicago-y-suburbios" target="_blank" >lugares en los 50 distritos y en 55 ubicaciones en el Condado de Cook suburbano abrieron</a> antes de las elecciones primarias del 17 de marzo. También se abrieron sitios de votación anticipada en los condados cercanos.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-7c0000" name="module-7c0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Marisel Hernández, presidenta de la Junta de Comisionados de Elecciones de Chicago, acompañada por la secretaria del Condado de Cook, Monica Gordon, estuvo en una conferencia de prensa en el centro de votación para actualizar a los votantes antes de las elecciones primarias. Las autoridades instaron a los votantes a votar anticipadamente para evitar problemas.</p><p>“Nunca sabes cómo estará el clima a principios de marzo, o qué circunstancias pueden surgir que te impidan votar como habías planeado”, dijo Hernández. “Estamos instando a los votantes de Chicago a votar temprano y disfrutar de la tranquilidad que viene con cumplir con tu deber cívico”.</p><p>Bobbi Ball, residente de Streeterville, pasó el lunes por el centro de votación para entregar su boleta de votación por correo. Los temas que más le preocupan incluyen la asequibilidad, la atención médica y el empleo.</p><p>“Creo que nos gustaría ir al supermercado y poder pagar por la comida, para que podamos comer”, dijo Ball, de 75 años. “Sería bueno que las personas que han perdido su seguro médico lo recuperen… Y sería bueno prestar atención a algunos de los errores que enfrenta nuestro país ahora”.</p><p>Rochelle Hamilton, residente de Hyde Park, votó en persona en el YMCA del lado sur en Woodlawn debido al escepticismo sobre la votación por correo.</p><p>“Antes de esto, creo que en los últimos 20 años, voté por correo. Pero ya no estoy segura de confiar en el sistema. Prefiero hacerlo en persona”, dijo.</p><p>Antes de las elecciones primarias, Hamilton dijo que le preocupa más encontrar candidatos que no acepten dinero del Comité de Asuntos Públicos de Estados Unidos-Israel (AIPAC).</p><p>“Para ser completamente honesta, voto por todos los candidatos anti-AIPAC. Eso es lo que más me importa, porque creo que muchos de los problemas que estamos teniendo ahora en la política exterior e incluso en la doméstica son por nuestra lealtad a un país extranjero”, agregó.</p><p>Y en el suburbio de Berwyn, lo primero en la mente de Danny Vilchez al ir el lunes fue la asequibilidad.</p><p>“El dinero ha sido el mayor problema que ha tenido mi familia, no sé desde hace cuánto”, dijo Vilchez. “Nos dijeron que todo sería más barato... Y todo no ha sido igual, si no peor”.</p><p>El joven de 22 años tuvo que transferirse de universidad el año pasado cuando los problemas económicos hicieron que asistir a su primera escuela ya no fuera viable. Con $13,000 en préstamos y planes para obtener una maestría en políticas públicas en otoño, dijo que perdió la fe en quienes ganaron en 2024 para cumplir con las promesas de un país más asequible.</p><p>Si bien se identificó como republicano cuando votó por primera vez en 2022, desde entonces ha cambiado de postura hacia la izquierda, en parte debido a la política de inmigración. Agregó que debería haber caminos más claros para obtener la ciudadanía.</p><p>“Mi familia es hispana, así que sentí que ya no éramos su demografía objetivo”, dijo Vilchez. “[Y] crecí en Chicago, así que me molesté mucho al ver las redadas en La Villita, específicamente”.</p><p>Se han presentado más de 170,500 solicitudes para votar por correo en Chicago y ya se han emitido más de 26,000 votos, destacó Hernández.</p><p>En comparación con las elecciones primarias de 2022, las autoridades dijeron que están viendo un aumento en el número de votos. En este momento de la primaria de junio de 2022, dijo que se habían emitido alrededor de 11,000 votos.</p><p>“En el momento de las elecciones los votantes miran el clima [político] en nuestro país y en nuestro estado y ciudad”, agregó Hernández.</p><p>También atribuyó el aumento en los votos al número de puestos vacantes en los que los titulares no se postulan para la reelección.</p><p>Los votantes han podido votar temprano en la oficina de la Junta de Elecciones de Chicago en el 69 W. Washington St. y desde el 12 de febrero en el supersitio en el centro de la ciudad ubicado en el 137 S. State St.</p><p>“No importa dónde vivas en la ciudad, ya sea en un lugar cercano a tu hogar, a la escuela o a tu lugar de trabajo, puedes elegir cualquier sitio de votación anticipada para emitir tu boleta”, dijo Hernández.</p><p>Encuentra una <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/how-to-vote-illinois-primary" target="_blank" >lista completa de sitios de votación anticipada aquí</a>. Los votantes que deseen votar por correo aún pueden inscribirse para obtener una boleta de voto por correo. La fecha límite para solicitarla es el 12 de marzo a las 5 p.m.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Violet Miller

entries.title: Bengals edge rusher Trey Hendrickson becomes free agent, could become a Bears target
entries.summary: <p>Add another edge rusher to the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears’ </a>free-agent watch list: Trey Hendrickson.</p><p>The Bengals didn’t issue the franchise tag to their star defensive end by Wednesday’s 3 p.m. deadline, which means Hendrickson will head to free agency next week.</p><p>Hendrickson is 31 and played just seven games for the Bengals last season because of injury — and after an offseason tug-of-war about his contract. He had 17 ½ sacks in each of the previous two seasons, though.</p><p>He said goodbye to Cincinnati fans, teammates and coaches in a long letter Tuesday afternoon.</p><p>Hendrickson played for <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/03/bears-drew-dalman-expected-to-retire-center-ryan-poles-falcons-stanford-nfl" target="_blank" >Bears</a> defensive coordinator Dennis Allen when the two worked for the Saints. Hendrickson played his first four seasons with the team after being drafted in 2017.</p><p>The Bears didn’t have any logical franchise tag candidates.</p><p>Those who were tagged include the Jets running back Breece Hall; Cowboys receiver George Pickens; Falcons tight end Kyle Pitts; and Colts quarterback Daniel Jones. Jones got the transition tag, which gives them the right to match any offer.</p><p>The franchise tag binds a player to their team for an extra season after calculating his salary based on top-performing players at his position. It’s often a starting point for a contract extension — tagged players can negotiate one until July 15.</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" entries.title: Out from the shadows, Chicago’s non-Equity stage managers take a turn in the spotlight
entries.summary: <p>Last year, Kyle Aschbrenner had to have a talk with his charges after one of them drew a penis on a bulletin board.</p><p>“I had to tell everyone to sit down and be like, ‘While I find this very funny, I have to tell you guys not to do stuff like this,’” Aschbrenner recalled.</p><p>Aschbrenner doesn’t run after-school detention at a high school for misfits. He’s a non-equity stage manager — perhaps the most stressful, complex, occasionally absurd, and under-appreciated job in Chicago theater.</p><p>Well, until recently. When the non-equity Jeff Awards are awarded on March 23, one stage manager will get some much-appreciated recognition. The Jeff nominees were announced Feb. 4; the <a class="Link" href="https://www.broadwayworld.com/chicago/article/Non-Equity-Nominees-Revealed-for-The-Jeff-Impresario-Award-20260303" target="_blank" ><u>stage manager nominees are out today. </u></a></p><p>The first non-equity stage manager award (along with the equity award) came in 2025 and went to Aschbrenner.</p><p>“I’m not really one for accolades — that’s not why I do it. But it was really humbling winning it and being recognized for the efforts because everything under the sun becomes your responsibility when you’re the stage manager,” said Aschbrenner, who received the award for his work with Blank Theatre Company’s 2024 season shows, “A Bright Room Called Day” and “On The Twentieth Century.”</p><p>There are somewhere between 60 and 70 non-equity theater companies in Chicago (about twice the number of equity ones) — from storefront stages that seat perhaps two dozen people to theaters such as Arlington Heights’ Metropolis, with a capacity of 329. The non-equity stage is typically where people who want to work in theater get their start; the actors aren’t part of the industry’s Actors’ Equity labor union and wages and benefits tend to be lower.</p><p>The stage manager is the glue that holds the production together, taking copious notes in their “prompt book” — detailing the places and movements of actors on stage, the sound and light cues and the set changes (among many other things). They also schedule and run rehearsals.</p><p>The stage manager inhabits the shadows — watching, tweaking, sweating the small stuff, and taking charge when the show is out of rehearsals and in front of a paying audience.</p><p>And if the stage manager is doing their job correctly, you won’t know that they are there.</p><p>They are both indispensable and dispensable.</p><p>“In college, they told us to make sure our prompt book … was up to date, so that if I get hit by a bus, somebody would be able to pick up my book and call the show in my absence,” said Shelby Burgus, a Chicago-area stage manager since 2013.</p><p>Blank Theatre’s co-artistic director Dustin Rothbart, who nominated Aschbrenner for the Jeff, said good stage managers are hard to come by because many are inexperienced (hired straight out of college) — and the really good ones quickly get lured away to equity theater.</p><p>And then there are those who see the job only as a means to an end, Rothbart said.</p><p>“When it becomes apparent in the rehearsal room that your stage manager would rather be on stage or the one giving the direction instead of doing the job they were hired for, that can often create some toxicity in the room,” Rothbart said.</p><p>He described Aschbrenner as “the best stage manager I’ve ever worked with,” calling him “extremely organized,” stern when he needs to be and willing to fill in for a sick fellow stage manager at a moment’s notice.</p><p>John Glover, the immediate past chair of the Jeff Awards’ executive committee, pushed hard for the non-equity stage manager award.</p><p>“They are a key element to the success of any production,” said Glover, who says he sees about 150 stage shows annually in the Chicago area. “Their work starts well before the first read-through and goes well beyond closing night. The job that they have is extraordinary.”</p><p>A Google search probably won’t turn it up, but the job description might also include: garbage collector, lightbulb changer, ripped pants fixer, therapist, nurse.</p><p>Becky Warner has been a Chicago-area stage manager for 13 years. During her very first gig, a lead actor told her 10 minutes before he had to go on that he was feeling ill.</p><p>“Had I been more experienced, I would have cancelled the show before we even started because he was that sick,” Warner said.</p><p>Just as the actor was about to go on, Warner saw him in the wings shaking and unable to walk. She hurried to the director to tell him to cancel the show. Then Warner and the director rushed the actor to the nearest emergency room. “He was severely dehydrated on top of having the flu,” Warner said.</p><p>And what does a stage manager get paid for a job that lasts about three months? Anywhere from $500 to about $1,200, local stage managers told the Sun-Times. A stage manager can expect to devote 20 or so hours a week to the job, one that doesn’t typically come with overtime pay or health benefits.</p><p>“I’ve been offered $300; I’ve never taken those,” Aschbrenner said.</p><p>Of the four stage managers with whom the Sun-Times spoke, all said they needed a day job to make ends meet, to make it possible to watch the magic happen when the lights go down and the curtain goes up.</p><p>“There is something so special about being in the room when these actors discover their characters and watching that process blossom and grow until I press the button to make the lights and sound happen and hear the audience’s reaction,” Burgus said.</p>
entries.author: Stefano Esposito
